entries.key: Smoke / test harness is wired
entries.what: Asserts 1+1==2 — a trivial, always-true check with no DSP or plugin code involved.
entries.why: The canonical harness-wiring canary: if even this fails, the test runner/build/JUCE UnitTest registration itself is broken, not any specific feature.
entries.deviationMeans: The test harness itself is broken (build, linking, or JUCE UnitTest registration) — do not investigate DSP correctness until this passes again, since no other test result can be trusted while the harness itself is suspect.

entries.key: ParamSnapshot / defaults match expected values
entries.what: Reads a fresh APVTS snapshot and asserts specific default values: oscCoarse=0, svfCutoffHz=1000, svfResonance=0.2, ampSustain=0.8, masterGainDb=-9.0, oscWaveform=0.
entries.why: Pins the exact factory-default patch sound — since preset backward-compatibility is explicitly not a constraint (per the standing decision), these literal defaults ARE the closest thing to a contract for what a freshly-loaded plugin sounds like.
entries.deviationMeans: A default parameter value has changed — since there's no preset-compat safety net, this changes what every user hears on first load; confirm it's an intentional default-tuning change, not an accidental default drift.

entries.key: ParamSnapshot / setting a parameter changes the snapshot
entries.what: Sets layer 0's filterCutoff to 2500 Hz via the APVTS parameter and asserts a fresh snapshot reads svfCutoffHz=2500 within 1.0.
entries.why: Baseline read-path correctness — the snapshot must reflect live parameter changes, since ParamSnapshot is the value-type handoff every DSP-side consumer (Layer::updateParameters) actually reads from.
entries.deviationMeans: Changing an APVTS parameter is not reaching the snapshot that DSP code reads — a UI/automation change to a parameter would silently fail to affect the actual sound.

entries.key: ParamSnapshot / snapshot carries spine model + separation
entries.what: Sets spineSeparation to 0.5 via APVTS and asserts the snapshot's spineSeparationOct reads 0.5 (within 0.01) and spineModel reads 0 (default Huggett).
entries.why: Confirms the spine-level parameters (filter model selection, separation) added by ADR-0011/Q14 correctly flow through the same snapshot mechanism as the older, pre-spine-library parameters.
entries.deviationMeans: The spine model selector or separation parameter isn't reaching the snapshot — live filter-model switching (Q17) or separation (Q14) could silently stop responding to UI/automation changes.

entries.key: ParamSnapshot / new v5.0 HP pre-filter + post-drive params default sanely
entries.what: Asserts hpCutoffHz defaults to 0 (HP off), huggettPostDrive defaults to 0, hpSlope defaults to 0 (12 dB), hpResonance defaults to 0; then sets the HP resonance param to its raw max (1.0) and asserts the snapshot value CAPS at 0.15 (documented: full-range OTA HP self-oscillates too hot).
entries.why: Pins both the v5.0 HP-pre-filter/post-drive default values AND a deliberate non-linear parameter mapping (the HP resonance knob's top end maps to 0.15, not 1.0) that exists specifically to keep the HP stage from being unusably harsh at max resonance.
entries.deviationMeans: Either the HP/post-drive defaults have drifted, or — more subtly — the HP resonance knob's deliberate ceiling-capping mapping has been removed/changed, which would let the HP stage self-oscillate far hotter than the design intends when a user turns the knob to max.

entries.key: ParamSnapshot / spine.huggett.routing snapshots and defaults to 0 (LP)
entries.what: Asserts huggettRouting defaults to 0 (LP), then sets the raw param to 7.0 and asserts the snapshot round-trips to routing=7 (LP+HP).
entries.why: Confirms the routing selector for Huggett's nine-routing set (Q14) both defaults sensibly (plain LP) and correctly transmits a non-default routing index through the snapshot.
entries.deviationMeans: The Huggett routing selector either defaults to the wrong mode or fails to transmit a selected routing to the DSP-facing snapshot — one of the nine Q14 routings could become unreachable from the UI/automation.

entries.key: ParamSnapshot / spine.modelFadeMs default reaches the snapshot (25 ms) and round-trips
entries.what: Asserts spineModelFadeMs defaults to 25.0 ms in the snapshot, then sets the raw param to 60.0 and asserts the snapshot reads 60.0 (within 0.1).
entries.why: Pins Q17's resolved default crossfade duration (~25 ms) and confirms the automatable spine.modelFadeMs parameter (2-100 ms range per Q17) correctly reaches the snapshot the hot-swap crossfade code actually uses.
entries.deviationMeans: Either the default crossfade duration has drifted from Q17's resolved 25 ms, or changing the fade-duration parameter no longer reaches the DSP — live model switches could use the wrong (possibly clicky, if too short) fade time.

entries.key: ParamSnapshot / Moog bank params exist with correct defaults
entries.what: Asserts the four Moog-bank APVTS parameters (spine.moog.mode, bassAmount, bassWave, bassOctave) exist, and the snapshot's moogMode defaults to 0 with moogBassAmount exactly +/-0 (FP_ZERO).
entries.why: Per-model namespaced parameter banks (the ADR-0008 idiom referenced by L7) must actually be registered for Moog — a missing parameter would mean part of Moog's UI (e.g. the sub-osc bass controls) has no backing automation-capable parameter at all.
entries.deviationMeans: One of Moog's bank parameters is missing from the APVTS — the corresponding UI control (e.g. bass wave/octave selector) would have nothing to bind to, or would silently fail to automate.

entries.key: Oscillator / sine at 440Hz has FFT peak near bin 440 (1Hz res, 48k samples)
entries.what: Renders 1 second (48000 samples, 1 Hz bin resolution) of a 440 Hz sine and brute-force-DFTs bins 430-450; asserts the peak-magnitude bin is within 1 bin of 440.
entries.why: Baseline pitch-accuracy guarantee for the core Oscillator — every voice's fundamental frequency correctness depends on this being right, independent of any filter/envelope/drive behavior tested elsewhere.
entries.deviationMeans: The oscillator's frequency-to-pitch mapping has drifted by more than ~1 Hz at 440 Hz — every note played would sound detectably out of tune, a foundational regression.

entries.key: Oscillator / saw at 1kHz has measurable energy at 2kHz, 3kHz harmonics
entries.what: Renders a 1 kHz saw and measures magnitude at 1k/2k/3k Hz; asserts the 2nd harmonic exceeds 20% and the 3rd exceeds 15% of the fundamental's magnitude (generous tolerance around the saw's theoretical 1/n harmonic falloff).
entries.why: Confirms the saw waveform genuinely carries harmonic content (not degenerated to a near-sine) — many tests elsewhere (AlgorithmRoutingTests, MultiLayerTests' drive test) deliberately use saw/harmonic-rich notes to make waveshaper/filter effects audible, and depend on this harmonic richness existing.
entries.deviationMeans: The saw oscillator has lost harmonic content (e.g. degenerated toward a sine) — any test elsewhere relying on saw's harmonics to make an effect measurable could produce false negatives unrelated to the actual feature being tested.

entries.key: Oscillator / reset returns phase to zero
entries.what: Runs 100 samples of a 440 Hz sine, calls reset(), and asserts the very next sample is within 1e-3 of zero (phase reset to the sine's zero-crossing start).
entries.why: Voice-retriggering (a new noteOn on a reused voice slot) depends on phase reset being clean — a stale phase would produce an audible click or wrong-sounding attack on retrigger.
entries.deviationMeans: Resetting the oscillator no longer returns it to a clean phase-zero start — retriggered notes (voice reuse, voice stealing) could click or start from an unexpected phase.

entries.key: Oscillator / zero frequency produces silence (or DC) without exploding
entries.what: Sets frequency to 0.0 on a saw oscillator and renders a block; asserts every sample stays bounded within 1.5 (whatever the exact zero-frequency behavior, it must not diverge).
entries.why: Zero frequency is a degenerate-but-reachable edge case (e.g. a MIDI note mapped to 0 Hz by a bug elsewhere, or an uninitialized state) that must fail safely rather than producing a runaway/NaN output.
entries.deviationMeans: The oscillator produces unbounded output at zero frequency — a degenerate parameter value that should be harmless is instead capable of blowing up a voice's output.

entries.key: Oscillator / two consecutive blocks produce continuous output (no glitch at boundary)
entries.what: Renders two consecutive 512-sample blocks and measures the sample-to-sample delta across the block boundary; asserts it's under 0.1 (a smooth continuation, not a phase jump).
entries.why: Block-based rendering (the JUCE processBlock convention) must not introduce discontinuities at block boundaries — a phase-tracking bug that only manifests across calls (not within a single long buffer) would otherwise go undetected.
entries.deviationMeans: The oscillator's phase state isn't correctly carrying over between processBlock calls — audio would click or glitch at every block boundary during real-time playback, though it might look fine in a single-buffer offline test.

entries.key: Envelope / starts at zero and is idle
entries.what: After prepare()+setParameters(attack=0.01s, decay=0.1s, sustain=0.5, release=0.2s) with no noteOn, asserts nextSample() returns 0.0 (within 1e-6) and isActive() is false.
entries.why: Baseline idle-state guarantee for the amp envelope — the one modulation source shipped today (per Q24, full LFO/mod-envelope basics are still scheduled); a voice must be silent before any note-on.
entries.deviationMeans: A freshly-prepared, un-triggered envelope is producing non-zero output or reporting itself active — every idle voice would leak audio or be incorrectly counted as busy (e.g. by voice-stealing logic).

entries.key: Envelope / attack ramps from 0 to ~1 over the attack time
entries.what: After noteOn(), steps exactly attackSamples (0.01s * SR) samples then asserts the next sample exceeds 0.9 — the attack stage reaches near-unity within its configured time.
entries.why: Pins the attack-stage timing contract that every voice's amplitude envelope relies on for correct note-onset loudness and timing.
entries.deviationMeans: The attack stage is too slow (soft onset) or overshooting — notes would sound different (mushier or clicky) at attack regardless of any filter/oscillator change.

entries.key: Envelope / decay falls toward sustain
entries.what: Runs a full second of samples after attack and asserts the envelope settles to the configured sustain level (0.5) within 0.05.
entries.why: Confirms the decay stage actually reaches and holds at the sustain plateau rather than overshooting, undershooting, or continuing to decay indefinitely.
entries.deviationMeans: Sustained notes are holding at the wrong loudness — decay either doesn't reach sustain or doesn't stop at it, audible as notes fading out (or staying too loud) when they should be steady.

entries.key: Envelope / noteOff drops to zero by end of release
entries.what: Calls noteOff() then steps 3x the configured release time (0.2s) worth of samples; asserts the envelope reaches 0.0 within 1e-3 and isActive() is false.
entries.why: Guarantees a released note actually finishes and frees its voice — this is what lets voice-stealing/voice-count logic (VoiceManager, the 64-voice Q2 target) trust that isActive() eventually goes false.
entries.deviationMeans: Released notes never fully silence or never report inactive — voices could get stuck 'active' forever, starving the 64-voice pool (Q2) of voices to steal for new notes.

entries.key: Envelope / reset clears state
entries.what: Triggers noteOn, advances one sample, calls reset(), and asserts the envelope is back to 0.0 output and inactive, as if freshly constructed.
entries.why: reset() is the hard state-clear used when a voice is reclaimed/re-bound (e.g. voice stealing, hot-swap re-bind per Q17); it must fully erase in-flight envelope state, not just partially.
entries.deviationMeans: reset() leaves residual envelope state — a reclaimed voice could start its new note already partway through an old envelope stage, causing an audible click or wrong initial loudness.

entries.key: Waveshaper / mix=0 passes input unchanged
entries.what: With drive=1.0 but mix=0.0, processes a 5-sample buffer and asserts output matches input exactly (within 1e-6).
entries.why: mix is the dry/wet blend control — at mix=0 the shaper must be a true bypass regardless of how extreme the drive setting is, since a user could crank drive expecting no effect until they raise mix.
entries.deviationMeans: The waveshaper is leaking driven signal through even at mix=0 — a user with drive cranked but mix at zero (expecting no audible effect) would hear unwanted coloration.

entries.key: Waveshaper / mix=1 drive=0 passes input nearly unchanged for small signals
entries.what: With mix=1.0/drive=0.0, processes small-amplitude samples (<=0.1) and asserts output matches input within 0.05 (a looser tolerance than the mix=0 case, since drive=0 is 'nearly linear' rather than an exact bypass).
entries.why: At drive=0 the shaper curve should approximate a straight line for small signals — this is the 'off' reference point for the drive control, distinct from mix's exact bypass.
entries.deviationMeans: The shaper curve at drive=0 is introducing more than expected coloration on small signals — the 'drive off' reference point has drifted from being effectively linear.

entries.key: Waveshaper / output is bounded
entries.what: Drives the shaper at drive=1.0/mix=1.0 with an extreme constant input (5.0) for 1024 samples; asserts every output sample stays within [-1, 1].
entries.why: The waveshaper block must be self-limiting regardless of how hot its input is — a core safety property for a block sitting inside the VAST algorithm graph ahead of the spine filter, where an unbounded shaper could feed a dangerously hot signal downstream.
entries.deviationMeans: The waveshaper can produce output outside [-1,1] under extreme drive — an unbounded shaper stage could feed a dangerously hot signal into the downstream spine filter/VCA chain.

entries.key: Waveshaper / odd symmetry: shaper(-x) == -shaper(x)
entries.what: At drive=0.7/mix=1.0, processes matched positive and negative input samples and asserts shaper(-x) equals -shaper(x) exactly (within 1e-6) for each pair.
entries.why: The waveshaper is specifically a SYMMETRIC (odd-harmonic-only) nonlinearity — this distinguishes it from the deliberately ASYMMETRIC AsymSaturator stages used in the spine (Q15), confirming the algorithm-graph shaper block and the spine drive stages are intentionally different characters.
entries.deviationMeans: The waveshaper has lost its odd symmetry (become asymmetric) — this would change it from a clean odd-harmonic distortion character to something closer to the spine's deliberately-asymmetric AsymSaturator, likely an unintended character change for this specific block.

entries.key: Voice / idle voice renders nothing
entries.what: Renders a block from a prepared-but-never-triggered Voice and asserts every output sample is within 1e-6 of zero.
entries.why: Baseline silence guarantee at the Voice level (below VoiceManager/Program) — the most granular unit in the signal chain must be provably silent before any note-triggering behavior is layered on top.
entries.deviationMeans: A Voice that has never received a noteOn is producing audio — the most fundamental silence guarantee in the entire signal chain is broken, and every higher-level silence test (VoiceManager, PluginLifecycle) built on this assumption would also be suspect.

entries.key: Voice / noteOn produces non-zero output
entries.what: Triggers noteOn(69, A4), renders two blocks (to get past attack), and asserts the summed absolute output exceeds 1.0.
entries.why: Baseline positive-path check that a Voice actually produces audio after being triggered — the counterpart to the idle-silence test.
entries.deviationMeans: A triggered voice is not producing audible output — the most basic note-to-sound path is broken at the Voice level, before any Layer/VoiceManager complexity is involved.

entries.key: Voice / noteOn renders finite, audible output (bind path)
entries.what: Using a freshly-prepared Layer/Voice pair (distinct instances from the main test's shared ones), triggers noteOn and renders one block; asserts every sample is finite and the peak magnitude is nonzero.
entries.why: Specifically exercises the voice's initial 'bind' path (a fresh voice's first note, as opposed to the shared/reused Layer instance in the earlier tests) — the P0 heap-use-after-free bug class (per InPlaceStateTests/LayerTests) specifically involves per-voice state binding.
entries.deviationMeans: A voice's FIRST bind on a note is producing non-finite or silent output — given the P0 heap-use-after-free history around per-voice state binding, treat any regression here as a potential memory-safety issue, not just an audio bug.

entries.key: Voice / factor 1 render produces finite audible output
entries.what: Prepares a Voice with the explicit 3-argument prepare(sr, block, factor=1) signature and renders two blocks after noteOn; asserts every sample on both channels is finite and each channel's summed absolute output is nonzero.
entries.why: Confirms the oversampling-aware 3-arg prepare/render signature behaves correctly at the identity factor (1x) — the baseline case every higher OS factor is compared against.
entries.deviationMeans: The oversampling-aware Voice API is broken even at factor 1 (the simplest, most common case) — since every OS-tier test builds on this code path, a regression here would likely affect every OS factor, not just os1.

entries.key: Voice / factor 4 render produces finite audible output
entries.what: Configures a hot patch (cutoff 8000 Hz, resonance 0.9 self-osc region, waveshaper + spine drive both engaged) at OS factor 4, renders 5 blocks (clearing the ~54-sample factor-4 latency plus envelope attack), and asserts every sample is finite with nonzero output on both channels.
entries.why: Proves a real, hot, nonlinear-path-engaging patch (not just a quiet sine) survives 4x oversampling through the full Voice render path — the OS tiers exist specifically to handle driven/resonant content, so testing them with a tame patch would miss the interesting failure modes.
entries.deviationMeans: A hot, resonant, driven patch is producing non-finite or silent output at 4x oversampling — since this deliberately exercises the self-oscillation and drive-nonlinearity regions together with OS, this is a strong signal of an OS-path interaction bug with the nonlinear DSP, not just a basic OS-plumbing issue.

entries.key: Voice / noteOff eventually silences voice
entries.what: After noteOff, renders up to 200 blocks (breaking early if isActive() goes false) and asserts the voice ends up inactive.
entries.why: Direct Voice-level guarantee (one level below VoiceManager's version) that a released note eventually frees its voice — essential for voice-stealing and the fixed 64-voice pool (Q2) to function, since a voice that never reports inactive can never be reclaimed.
entries.deviationMeans: A voice is not becoming inactive after release completes — this voice would be permanently unavailable for reuse, silently shrinking the effective polyphony below the intended 64-voice cap (Q2) over a long playing session.

entries.key: VoiceManager / no MIDI input â silent output
entries.what: Renders a block with no MIDI events and asserts summed absolute output is within 1e-6 of zero.
entries.why: Baseline silence guarantee at the VoiceManager/Program level (one layer up from PluginLifecycleTests' processor-level version) - no notes playing must mean true silence through the full voice-allocation stack.
entries.deviationMeans: VoiceManager is producing audio with no active notes - a voice-allocation or leftover-voice-state bug at the manager level, distinct from (but analogous to) the processor-level silence guarantee.

entries.key: VoiceManager / noteOn produces non-silent output
entries.what: Sends a noteOn (MIDI 60), renders a block, clears MIDI, renders a second block, and asserts the second block's summed absolute output exceeds 1.0.
entries.why: Confirms VoiceManager correctly allocates and sustains a voice across multiple render calls after a single noteOn event, not just within the same block the event arrived in.
entries.deviationMeans: VoiceManager either fails to allocate a voice for a new note, or fails to sustain it into subsequent render calls — a note might sound only in the exact block it started, or not at all.

entries.key: VoiceManager / noteOff releases voice
entries.what: After a noteOff, renders 200 blocks (draining the release envelope) and asserts the final block's summed absolute output is under 1e-3 (fully released).
entries.why: Confirms the VoiceManager-level integration of noteOff-to-envelope-release actually silences a voice over time — the manager-level counterpart to EnvelopeTests' unit-level release-to-zero guarantee.
entries.deviationMeans: A note-off is not fully silencing its voice at the VoiceManager level — a released note could ring out indefinitely (or nearly so), which would also starve the voice pool if isActive() correspondingly never goes false.

entries.key: VoiceManager / more notes than polyphony triggers voice stealing
entries.what: Sends kNumVoices+2 simultaneous noteOn events (more than the polyphony cap) in one block; asserts the render completes without crashing and every output sample is finite.
entries.why: Voice-stealing (reallocating an already-playing voice to a new note when the polyphony cap is exceeded) is a mandatory behavior given the fixed 64-voice cap (Q2) — this proves the manager handles the over-allocation case safely, not just that it happens to work under the cap.
entries.deviationMeans: Exceeding the polyphony cap is producing a crash or non-finite output — since Q2 fixes the voice cap at 64 (not unlimited), voice-stealing under real playing conditions (e.g. a dense chord or sustain-pedal buildup) must never destabilize the plugin.

entries.key: PluginLifecycle / construct -> prepare -> silent processBlock
entries.what: Constructs the processor, calls prepareToPlay, and processes one block with no MIDI input; asserts the summed absolute output across both channels is within 1e-6 of zero.
entries.why: The most basic plugin-host-contract smoke test — a freshly-loaded plugin instance with no notes must be silent, not producing noise/DC/garbage from uninitialized state.
entries.deviationMeans: A freshly-instantiated plugin is producing noise with no notes played — likely uninitialized DSP state, which would be audible the instant the plugin loads in a host, before any note is even played.

entries.key: PluginLifecycle / noteOn through processBlock produces audible output
entries.what: Sends a noteOn (MIDI 69) through processBlock, renders 4 more blocks to get past attack, and asserts summed absolute output exceeds 1.0.
entries.why: The full-stack (host-facing AudioProcessor::processBlock, not just Voice::render) smoke test that a note actually produces sound through the entire plugin, including MIDI parsing and the processor-level render path.
entries.deviationMeans: The plugin is not producing sound in response to MIDI note-on through its actual host-facing processBlock entry point — this is more encompassing than a DSP-unit-level failure; even if internal Layer/Voice tests pass, something in the processor's MIDI-to-render wiring is broken.

entries.key: PluginLifecycle / state save and restore round-trips a non-default parameter
entries.what: Sets layer 0's filterCutoff to 3200 Hz, calls getStateInformation, loads that state into a FRESH second processor instance via setStateInformation, and asserts the restored cutoff reads ~3200 (within 5.0).
entries.why: The core host-persistence contract (DAW project save/reload, preset save/load) — a parameter change must survive a full serialize/deserialize round-trip into a different instance, independent of the explicitly-not-a-constraint preset BACKWARD-compat concern (this is same-version round-trip, not cross-version migration).
entries.deviationMeans: Saving and reloading plugin state is losing parameter values — every DAW project using this plugin would lose its settings on reload, a severe regression regardless of the preset-backcompat policy (which only concerns compat across VERSIONS, not within one).

entries.key: PluginLifecycle / safety limiter is enabled by default
entries.what: Asserts a freshly-constructed processor reports isLimiterEnabled()==true.
entries.why: The safety limiter is the last line of defense against hearing-damage-risk DSP blowups (per Q27's field report, it's what prevented harm during the pre-fix resonance defect) — it must be ON by default so a fresh install/session is protected even before a user consciously configures anything.
entries.deviationMeans: The safety limiter defaults to OFF — every fresh plugin instance would be unprotected against a DSP blow-up (like the pre-Q27-fix resonance defect) until a user manually enables it, a hearing-safety regression.

entries.key: PluginLifecycle / limiter-enable round-trips through saved state (OFF and ON)
entries.what: Disables the limiter, saves state, loads into a fresh instance (which starts ON), and asserts the loaded instance reads OFF; repeats for re-enabling to ON, asserting each explicit choice persists across save/restore.
entries.why: The limiter-enable flag being persistable is what lets a user's deliberate safety choice (e.g. someone who wants it off for a specific patch) survive a project reload, rather than silently reverting to the default every time.
entries.deviationMeans: The limiter's enabled/disabled state isn't persisting through save/restore — a user's explicit safety choice (on or off) would be silently overridden on project reload, which is a correctness bug either direction (unwanted protection removed, or unwanted protection re-added).

entries.key: PluginLifecycle / oversampling settings round-trip and resolve active factor
entries.what: Sets realtimeOS=2 and offlineOS=8, saves and restores state into a fresh instance, asserts both settings persist, and that activeOS() resolves to realtimeOS (2) in a non-realtime-false unit-test context.
entries.why: Oversampling tier selection (Q12's Light/Normal/Heavy/Full separate live+render selectors, deferred to v5.1) must persist per-project and correctly resolve which of the two settings (realtime vs offline) is 'active' based on host render mode.
entries.deviationMeans: Oversampling tier settings aren't persisting, or activeOS() is resolving to the wrong one of realtime/offline — a saved project could silently revert to the wrong OS tier (affecting both CPU cost and aliasing) on reload.

entries.key: PluginLifecycle / changing realtime OS updates reported latency
entries.what: Asserts getLatencySamples()==0 at OS=1, then setRealtimeOS(4) makes it equal VoiceOversampler::latencyBaseSamples(4), then back to OS=1 returns it to 0.
entries.why: Hosts need an accurate PDC (plugin delay compensation) latency report whenever the oversampling factor changes live — an incorrect report would misalign this plugin's output against other tracks in a DAW session.
entries.deviationMeans: Changing the realtime OS factor is no longer updating the reported plugin latency — a DAW host would misalign this track against others whenever the user changes OS tier, since PDC compensation would use a stale latency value.

entries.key: PluginLifecycle / enabled limiter caps a hot processed block; disabled does not
entries.what: Cranks master gain to +6 dB (enough to exceed a -12 dBFS ceiling) with the limiter enabled, plays a loud note, and asserts the resulting output peak stays at or below the ceiling (10^(-12/20)) within 1e-4 across 8 rendered blocks.
entries.why: End-to-end (full processor, not a bare SafetyLimiter unit test) proof that the safety limiter's ceiling is actually enforced on real, hot, multi-block audio through the complete signal chain — the practical guarantee behind the 'limiter defaults ON' policy.
entries.deviationMeans: A deliberately hot signal through the full plugin is exceeding the limiter's ceiling — the safety-limiter guarantee is broken in the actual host-facing signal path, not just in the isolated SafetyLimiterTests unit tests.

entries.key: AlgorithmLibrary / library trimmed to 2 algorithms; entry 0 is the shaper (spine carries the filter)
entries.what: Asserts AlgorithmLibrary::count()==2, entry 0's id is "shaper" with exactly 1 slot of BlockTypeId::Waveshaper.
entries.why: Documents and pins the L2/Q13-era trim of the VAST algorithm library down to 'shaper' + 'thru' now that the filter is a permanent spine feature rather than a per-algorithm block, closing off the pre-spine larger algorithm set.
entries.deviationMeans: The algorithm library's shape has changed (count, order, or block composition of entry 0) — confirm whether this is an intentional VAST-graph expansion (Q5/Q6) or an accidental regression of the spine/algorithm split.

entries.key: AlgorithmLibrary / ids are unique
entries.what: Pairwise-compares every AlgorithmLibrary entry's id string against every other, asserting no duplicates.
entries.why: Algorithm ids are used as stable lookup keys (AlgorithmLibrary::indexOfId) throughout params/presets; a duplicate would make lookups ambiguous.
entries.deviationMeans: Two algorithm table entries share an id string — indexOfId() would silently resolve to whichever comes first, misrouting any code that looked up the other one.

entries.key: AlgorithmLibrary / every algorithm is well-formed: known types, no duplicate type
entries.what: For every algorithm, asserts slotCount <= kMaxSlots, every blockTypePerSlot entry is a valid non-zero BlockTypeId, and no block type repeats within the same algorithm.
entries.why: The graph-execution code assumes each algorithm is a well-formed, duplicate-free slot list; malformed data here would either crash the renderer or silently double-process a block type.
entries.deviationMeans: An algorithm table entry has an out-of-range block type or repeats a block type in two slots — the DSP graph executor's assumptions about the data are violated and it may misbehave or crash.

entries.key: AlgorithmLibrary / thru algorithm is empty
entries.what: Asserts the 'thru' algorithm entry has slotCount==0.
entries.why: 'thru' is the explicit pass-through/no-op algorithm (the negative control used by AlgorithmRoutingTests); it must genuinely declare zero blocks, not merely behave like it does.
entries.deviationMeans: The 'thru' algorithm has picked up a slot — it is no longer a true pass-through, which would invalidate any test using it as a negative control.

entries.key: AlgorithmName / algoNames() count matches the library
entries.what: Asserts the UI-facing params::algoNames() StringArray has exactly as many entries as AlgorithmLibrary::count().
entries.why: The algorithm choice parameter (APVTS) and the trimmed 2-entry AlgorithmLibrary (shaper, thru) must stay in lockstep or the combo box and the DSP graph selection desync.
entries.deviationMeans: The UI parameter's choice list and the actual DSP algorithm table have drifted out of sync — selecting an algorithm by index in the UI could route to the wrong (or a nonexistent) DSP graph.

entries.key: AlgorithmName / entry 0 is 'Shaper' (the spine carries the filter)
entries.what: Asserts algoNames()[0] is exactly the string "Shaper".
entries.why: Encodes the L2/Q13 architectural decision that the filter now lives in the constant Summit spine, not as a selectable VAST algorithm block — algorithm 0 being 'Shaper' (not e.g. 'Filter+Shaper') is a visible marker that the split happened correctly.
entries.deviationMeans: The algorithm naming or ordering has changed at index 0 — check whether the spine/algorithm-graph split (L2, Q13) has regressed or whether this is an intentional rename that the test needs updating for.

entries.key: AlgorithmName / no name contains a lone 0xE2 byte (the ASCII-decode artefact)
entries.what: Scans every algorithm display name for a stray 0xE2 juce_wchar — the tell-tale first byte of a UTF-8 arrow (U+2192) that was decoded byte-per-char instead of as UTF-8, which renders as mojibake ('Filter â□□ Shaper').
entries.why: Guards the util::u8() UTF-8-centralization rule (feedback_utf8_centralized): juce::String(const char*) assumes ASCII and mangles bytes >127; algorithm names carrying a U+2192 arrow are exactly the kind of string that regresses if a new name bypasses util::u8() at the JUCE boundary.
entries.deviationMeans: A non-ASCII display name is reaching the UI through a raw juce::String(const char*) conversion instead of util::u8() — expect visible mojibake in the algorithm combo box.

entries.key: SpineFilter / LP cell passes lows, attenuates highs
entries.what: Measures a bare TptSvfCell (LP tap, cutoff 1000, res 0) at 100 Hz and 10000 Hz; asserts low magnitude exceeds 0.7 and high magnitude is under 0.1.
entries.why: The most primitive linear-core building-block sanity check — TptSvfCell is the base topology multiple higher-level filters (NlSvfCell, HuggettFilter) are built from, so this must be correct before anything layered on top can be trusted.
entries.deviationMeans: The most basic linear SVF cell has a broken LP shape — since this is the base topology for the higher-level nonlinear cells, a failure here would cascade into every filter that builds on it.

entries.key: SpineFilter / Huggett 24 dB LP attenuates an octave above cutoff more than 12 dB
entries.what: At cutoff 1000 Hz, probe 2000 Hz (one octave above), compares 12 dB vs 24 dB slope magnitude; asserts 24 dB reads strictly lower (steeper attenuation) than 12 dB.
entries.why: Independent, simpler check (bare HuggettFilter, no Layer/Voice wrapper) of the fundamental 12-vs-24-dB slope-ordering property already covered more thoroughly by HuggettSeparationTests' T2 — a quick sanity check at the SpineFilter unit-test level.
entries.deviationMeans: The 24 dB slope is no longer steeper than 12 dB at the most basic level — check whether HuggettSeparationTests' more detailed T2 slope-value test is also failing to determine scope.

entries.key: SpineFilter / SpineFilterSlot filters using the active model
entries.what: Wraps a HuggettFilter (LP24, cutoff 500) in a SpineFilterSlot and processes an 8000 Hz tone through slot.processStereo (rather than calling the filter directly); asserts the high-frequency peak is cut below 0.1.
entries.why: Proves the SpineFilterSlot wrapper (the hot-swap-capable indirection layer every voice actually renders through, per L7/Q17) correctly forwards to and filters through its bound model, not just that the underlying HuggettFilter works when called directly.
entries.deviationMeans: The SpineFilterSlot indirection layer is not correctly filtering through its bound model — even if the underlying filter model works when tested directly (as in the other tests in this file), the wrapper every real voice actually uses could be broken.

entries.key: HuggettNonlinear / AsymSaturator: disengaged at zero drive, engaged when driven
entries.what: Asserts AsymSaturator::engaged() is false at drive=0 and true once drive=0.5/bias=0.18 is set.
entries.why: engaged() is used by driven-path optimizations (e.g. bypass-when-off) that only matter if the stage genuinely reports its own bypass state correctly at zero drive.
entries.deviationMeans: The saturator's engagement flag is wrong — either it reports engaged at zero drive (wasting CPU or subtly coloring 'bypassed' signal) or fails to report engaged when actually driven (skipping processing that should happen).

entries.key: HuggettNonlinear / AsymSaturator: adds even harmonics (asymmetry) and is bounded
entries.what: Drives AsymSaturator at drive=1.0/bias=0.25 with a 220 Hz tone; asserts output peak stays below 2.0 (bounded) and that a nonzero DC accumulates over the second half of the buffer (the tell-tale of the asymmetric — not symmetric tanh — shaping).
entries.why: Q15 (white-box OTA vs gray-box tanh) resolved on THREE ASYMMETRIC tanh-class stages specifically because asymmetry is load-bearing for the Summit's 'dirty/edgy, not warm' character (per the huggett-filter-dossier); a symmetric shaper would fail this DC-offset check even if bounded.
entries.deviationMeans: The saturator has become symmetric (no DC/even-harmonic asymmetry) or unbounded — either loses the deliberately-chosen edgy Summit character (Q15) or reintroduces a runaway output.

entries.key: HuggettNonlinear / DcBlocker removes a constant offset, keeps audio
entries.what: Feeds a 200 Hz tone with a +0.5 DC offset through DcBlocker for 8192 samples; asserts the tail-averaged output has near-zero DC (<0.02) while the AC content's RMS stays above 0.5.
entries.why: DcBlocker cleans up the DC that asymmetric saturation stages (AsymSaturator, the resonance loop) deliberately introduce — it must remove the offset without also attenuating the audio signal riding on it.
entries.deviationMeans: The DC blocker is either leaving residual DC (which could accumulate through cascaded asymmetric stages) or attenuating legitimate audio along with the DC — a basic building-block regression that would affect every asymmetric drive stage downstream.

entries.key: HuggettNonlinear / DcBlocker keeps L/R state independent
entries.what: Feeds +0.5 DC to channel 0 and -0.5 DC to channel 1 of the same DcBlocker instance for 8192 samples each; asserts both channels independently converge toward zero DC (<0.05), which would fail if state were shared and the opposite signs cross-contaminated.
entries.why: A stereo DcBlocker with shared (not per-channel) state would let one channel's DC removal be corrupted by the other's opposite-sign offset — this is the guard against that specific state-sharing bug class.
entries.deviationMeans: The DC blocker's L/R state has become coupled — one channel's DC estimate is contaminating the other, meaning stereo signals with different per-channel DC content would be mishandled.

entries.key: HuggettNonlinear / NlSvfCell ~= linear at low resonance (no saturation drift)
entries.what: At resonance=0.1/resSat=0.1, sweeps 100 Hz and 10000 Hz through NlSvfCell (fc=1000) and asserts the LP shape is intact (low passes >0.7, high attenuates <0.1) — i.e. the nonlinear saturator isn't perceptibly coloring the response at low resonance.
entries.why: At low resonance the nonlinear resonance-saturator path should be nearly inert — this guards against the saturator introducing unwanted coloration or level drift even when the resonance knob is barely engaged.
entries.deviationMeans: The nonlinear saturator is coloring the filter's basic LP shape even at low resonance, where it should be nearly transparent — every filter setting, not just high-resonance ones, would sound subtly different from the linear baseline.

entries.key: HuggettNonlinear / NlSvfCell self-oscillation is bounded at max resonance
entries.what: Kicks an NlSvfCell at fc=800, res=1.0, resSat=1.0 with a unit impulse then runs 0.5s of silence; asserts no NaN/Inf and settled peak stays below 4.0.
entries.why: A second, independently-parameterized (fc=800 vs the other test's fc=1000) instance of the Q27 bounded-resonance guard, at the raw NlSvfCell level rather than through the full HuggettFilter/FUT wrapper.
entries.deviationMeans: Self-oscillation at max resonance is unbounded at this specific cutoff — narrows down whether a Q27-class regression is cutoff-dependent (compare against the HuggettBoundedResonanceTests fc=1000 case).

entries.key: HuggettNonlinear / HuggettFilter at zero drive/resonance == bare linear cell
entries.what: Compares HuggettFilter (LP, 12dB, sep=0, cutoff=1200, res=0, drive=0) sample-by-sample against a directly-configured reference NlSvfCell at the same cutoff/resonance; asserts max per-sample difference is under 1e-5 (bit-for-bit linear).
entries.why: Proves that at drive=0/resonance=0, all the nonlinear machinery wrapping the linear core (saturators, drive stages, separation logic) is a true no-op -- the full HuggettFilter degenerates EXACTLY to its underlying linear cell, not just approximately.
entries.deviationMeans: The 'off' state of the nonlinear wrapper is no longer a perfect no-op -- some nonlinear stage is leaking a tiny coloration into what should be the pure linear baseline, which would make every 'zero drive' measurement elsewhere in the suite slightly untrustworthy as a linear reference.

entries.key: HuggettNonlinear / HuggettFilter: driving changes harmonic content but stays bounded
entries.what: Runs a 220 Hz saw through HuggettFilter (LP24, res=0.3) at drive=0 vs drive=1.0; asserts driven output is non-silent, every sample stays under 4.0 (bounded), and the driven signal differs from clean by more than 0.01 average absolute difference (a crude harmonic-change proxy).
entries.why: Confirms the drive stages actually do something audible (harmonic enrichment) while never producing a runaway output — the combined-effect sanity check one level above the individual per-stage bounded-ness tests.
entries.deviationMeans: Either driving the filter no longer changes the sound (drive stage inert) or the driven output is unbounded (a Q27-adjacent runaway) — check which assertion actually failed.

entries.key: HuggettNonlinear / Huggett Notch attenuates the band around cutoff vs LP passband
entries.what: Compares HuggettFilter's Notch routing magnitude at the cutoff (1000 Hz) against its LP routing magnitude in the passband (100 Hz); asserts the Notch centre reads less than half the LP passband level.
entries.why: Notch is one of the nine dual-filter routings shipped in Q14/v5.2 and is the mode FilterUnderTestTests declares Huggett (uniquely, vs Moog) as supporting — this pins its basic notch shape (attenuation at centre, not at the band edges).
entries.deviationMeans: The Notch routing is no longer attenuating around its centre frequency — a regression in one of the nine Q14 dual-filter routings, specifically the one Huggett's FUT declares special support for.

entries.key: HuggettSeparation / T1: separation is alive in BOTH 12 dB and 24 dB (LP)
entries.what: For both 12 dB and 24 dB LP slopes, compares response curves at separation=0 vs separation=2.0 across 8 probe frequencies (250-6000 Hz) and asserts the max magnitude difference anywhere in the band exceeds 3 dB — deliberately NOT at one fixed probe, per the code comment, because the v5.2 click fix keeps 12 dB passband level stable while separation widens the band shape.
entries.why: Separation (the ±4 oct cutA/cutB split, Q14's stretch goal) must remain audible at both slopes; the multi-frequency-max approach specifically avoids a false negative from the v5.2 fix that intentionally removed a single-frequency level jump.
entries.deviationMeans: Separation has stopped reshaping the LP response at one of the two slopes — since the test already accounts for the v5.2 level-stability fix, a failure here is a genuine loss of the separation effect, not a level artifact.

entries.key: HuggettSeparation / T2: sep=0 single-mode slopes are correct (12 dB ~1-pole-pair, 24 dB ~2x steeper)
entries.what: Measures rolloff across one octave (2000->4000 Hz, an octave above the 1000 Hz cutoff) at separation=0 for both slopes; asserts 12 dB rolloff is 9-15 dB/oct and 24 dB rolloff is 21-27 dB/oct.
entries.why: Baseline slope-correctness check independent of separation — confirms the single-mode (sep=0) LP behaves like a genuine one-pole-pair (12 dB) vs two-pole-pair (24 dB) filter before any separation effects are layered on.
entries.deviationMeans: The basic slope steepness of one of the two LP modes has drifted outside the expected range — a fundamental filter-order regression, separate from anything separation-specific.

entries.key: HuggettSeparation / T3: two distinct resonances appear and pull apart with separation (ParLPLP)
entries.what: Measures ParLPLP routing (res=0.97) across 10 probe frequencies at separation=0 and separation=3.0, counting local response-curve peaks with >1 dB prominence; asserts sep=0 shows at most 1 peak (single combined resonance) and sep=3 shows at least 2 (the two cutoffs' resonances have pulled apart).
entries.why: This is the core sonic signature of Huggett's dual-SVF-cell separation design (Q13/Q14) — parallel LP+LP at high resonance should show ONE resonant peak when unified (sep=0) and visibly split into TWO as cutA/cutB diverge, which is what makes separation audibly interesting rather than just a subtle EQ tilt.
entries.deviationMeans: The dual-resonance-splitting behavior that defines Huggett's separation feature has broken — either the peaks never separate (feature inert at high sep) or a spurious second peak appears at sep=0 (feature leaking when it shouldn't be active).

entries.key: HuggettSeparation / T4: routing shapes (LP+HP complementary vs HP+HP low-rejecting)
entries.what: Measures ParLPHP (sep=2) at 200/1000/6000 Hz, asserting both low and high read more than 2 dB above the mid (a complementary/notch-like dip); and ParHPHP (sep=1) asserting 6000 Hz reads more than 12 dB above 150 Hz (low-rejecting).
entries.why: Different parallel routings (part of the nine-routing Q14 set) should produce qualitatively different, recognizable response shapes — LP+HP should complement (dip in the middle) while HP+HP should uniformly reject lows; this distinguishes real routing-specific behavior from all routings collapsing to the same generic shape.
entries.deviationMeans: Two of the nine Q14 routings (ParLPHP, ParHPHP) no longer produce their characteristic shapes — routing selection may not be correctly wiring the two filter cells' modes/mix.

entries.key: HuggettSeparation / T5: stable + finite at max resonance and max separation, all single modes
entries.what: Sweeps all combinations of {LP,BP,HP} x {12dB,24dB} x {separation -4,+4} at resonance=0.999 (near-max), running 64 blocks of 128 samples of a 220 Hz tone; asserts output stays finite throughout and peak never exceeds 100.0 (no runaway) for every combination.
entries.why: Broad stability sweep at the extremes of both resonance and separation across every single-mode/slope combination — the kind of combinatorial edge-case coverage that a Q27-class defect (found in one specific configuration) could easily hide in an untested corner of.
entries.deviationMeans: One of the 12 mode/slope/separation-sign combinations at near-max resonance is unstable — narrows down exactly which configuration corner has a stability defect, useful for triaging a Q27-adjacent regression.

entries.key: HuggettSeparationClick / LP/HP separation is continuous (click-free) across sep=0 in both slopes
entries.what: For LP (probe 200 Hz) and HP (probe 6000 Hz) modes, at both 12 dB and 24 dB slopes, compares passband RMS at separation=0 vs separation=0.02; asserts the level jump across that boundary is under 1.0 dB in every one of the 4 combinations.
entries.why: Regression for 'Separation clicks when going through 0' — a single->parallel-sum topology switch at sep=0 previously showed as an audible passband level jump; this pins continuity across that specific knob boundary for both filter modes and both slopes.
entries.deviationMeans: Turning the separation knob through zero produces an audible level jump (a click) again — the topology-switch discontinuity has regressed in one of the 4 mode/slope combinations tested.

entries.key: HuggettDriveOnset / post-drive fades in: a hair off 0 barely differs from bypassed
entries.what: Measures steady-state passband RMS of a Huggett LP24 with postDrive=0.02 vs postDrive=0 (bypassed); asserts the difference is under 0.5 dB.
entries.why: Regression for 'drive jumps abruptly off 0 instead of fading in' — the old code engaged the post-drive shaper at a fixed character (bias 0.15 + ~1.8 dB makeup) the instant drive>0, producing an audible jump at the first knob increment.
entries.deviationMeans: Post-drive is once again snapping to an audible character change at the very first increment off zero, instead of fading in gradually — an onset-click regression in the post-drive stage.

entries.key: HuggettDriveOnset / pre-drive fades in: a hair off 0 barely differs from bypassed
entries.what: Same onset-jump measurement as the post-drive case but for pre-drive=0.02 vs 0; asserts under 0.5 dB difference from bypassed.
entries.why: Mirrors the post-drive onset guard for the pre-drive (input) stage — both drive stages had the same class of onset-discontinuity bug.
entries.deviationMeans: Pre-drive is snapping to an audible character change at the first knob increment instead of fading in — mirrors the post-drive onset regression but in the input stage.

entries.key: HuggettBoundedResonance / forced response at max resonance is bounded (single cell)
entries.what: Drives a single NlSvfCell at fc=1000 Hz, resonance=1.0, resSat=1.0 with a sustained -6 dBFS tone AT the cutoff for 1 second; asserts output stays finite throughout and the settled peak is below 6.0 (~+15 dBFS).
entries.why: Direct regression test for the Q27 defect: pre-fix, the transposed satRes operands caused anti-damping that let output run away to +22..+89 dBFS under sustained drive; this is the single-cell forced-response bound the fix guarantees.
entries.deviationMeans: The resonance loop is expanding again under sustained forced drive — this is the Q27 defect returning; treat as a hearing-safety regression (the near-miss field report was 'sound disappears then re-appears', consistent with state blow-up), not a voicing tweak.

entries.key: HuggettBoundedResonance / max resonance genuinely self-oscillates (sustained whistle, rail-limited)
entries.what: Kicks an NlSvfCell at max resonance (fc=1000, res=1.0, resSat=1.0) and runs 3 seconds; asserts the whistle is finite throughout, sustains (late-window peak > 0.05) without decaying to less than half its early-window peak, and stays below amplitude 6.0 (rail-limited, not runaway).
entries.why: Distinguishes the authorized behavior (genuine, bounded self-oscillation at max resonance — an analog-authentic property) from both failure modes: the pre-fix anti-damping blow-up AND an over-correction that would kill the whistle entirely (the code comment notes an early fix attempt 'removed it entirely, kicked ring decayed').
entries.deviationMeans: Either the filter no longer self-oscillates at max resonance (an over-correction regression, killing a real Summit-authentic behavior) or it's oscillating without bound (the Q27 defect returning) — check both directions.

entries.key: HuggettBoundedResonance / first resonance increment is click-free (continuous engagement)
entries.what: Runs a twin-signal difference test: identical hot (1.6 amplitude) 400 Hz signal, one run holds resonance at 0 throughout, the other steps resonance from 0 to 0.02 mid-stream; asserts the difference burst in the 5ms right after the step doesn't exceed 2x the settled (80-100ms later) steady-state difference.
entries.why: Regression for a second field bug found alongside Q27: the nonlinear resonance path used to engage BINARILY at the first res>0 increment (state rails snapping to full strength instantly), producing an audible click and sudden character change rather than continuous engagement.
entries.deviationMeans: Turning the resonance knob up from zero produces an audible click or abrupt character jump at the very first increment — the nonlinear engagement has regressed from continuous to binary.

entries.key: HuggettBoundedResonance / gain does not grow with input level (monotone non-expansion, LP24 res 0.9)
entries.what: Measures the Huggett FUT's gain at the resonant peak (~999.5 Hz, LP24, res=0.9) at -60 dBFS and -6 dBFS input; asserts gain(-6dBFS) does not exceed gain(-60dBFS)+1.0 dB (monotone non-expansion), and that output peak at -6 dBFS stays below +20 dBFS.
entries.why: This is the exact quantitative regression the Q27 large-signal read measured: pre-fix, gain rose from +61 dB (small-signal) to +86 dB (at -6 dBFS) — a ~25 dB expansion that no physical OTA filter produces; post-fix measured gain@-6dBFS = +8 dB (was +86).
entries.deviationMeans: The filter is expanding gain with input level again at its resonant peak — this is precisely the Q27 defect signature (the 'self-limiting resonance saturator anti-limits' bug); the authentic compression LAW still awaits SP-D hardware calibration, but this test guards the defect fix, not the final voicing.

entries.key: OverdriveDiagnostic / AsymSaturator (plain tanh): aliasing vs drive, matches inline reference
entries.what: Diagnostic (not gating): for amplitudes {0.7,1.5} x drives {0,0.3,0.6,1.0}, prints peak and inharmonic-energy-ratio (an aliasing/artifact score) for both AsymSaturator and an inline reference tanh implementation; asserts only finiteness and that the two match within 1e-6 (the actual pass/fail is the equivalence check, not the printed diagnostics).
entries.why: Seeds a per-component diagnostic platform (per the file's header comment) that reports WHERE crackle/aliasing artifacts come from via an objective inharmonic-energy metric, rather than relying on listening — this specific test's gating assertion also guards that AsymSaturator hasn't silently diverged from its own simple reference derivation.
entries.deviationMeans: If the equivalence assertion fails, AsymSaturator's actual implementation has diverged from its documented plain-tanh formula — read the printed inharmonic-dB table to see if a change also affected the artifact profile, since that's diagnostic output, not gated.

entries.key: OverdriveDiagnostic / HuggettFilter post-drive path: per-block (128) vs per-sample
entries.what: Diagnostic: compares HuggettFilter's post-drive output processed in one-sample-at-a-time calls vs 128-sample blocks, printing peak/inharmonic-dB/max-diff for amplitudes {0.5,2.0} x postDrive {0.5,1.0}; asserts only finiteness.
entries.why: Investigates whether per-block coefficient/state updates (vs true per-sample) introduce a measurable artifact — the file's header explains a per-block coefficient 'click' would show up as energy at the block rate, which is inharmonic and thus visible in the inharmonic-energy score even though this test doesn't gate on it.
entries.deviationMeans: A failure here means non-finite output, not necessarily an artifact regression — to assess actual block-size sensitivity, read the printed maxDiff-blk-vs-sample and inharmonic-dB columns; a growing gap would suggest the block-processing path is introducing audible artifacts the per-sample path doesn't have.

entries.key: OverdriveDiagnostic / HuggettHpStage: resonant peak structure (cutoff 1000 Hz, db24, drive 0)
entries.what: Diagnostic: prints a frequency-response table (17 probe frequencies 100Hz-20kHz) for HuggettHpStage at 3 resonance settings (0.15, 0.5, 1.0); asserts only expect(true) per row — this is a reporting tool, not a gate.
entries.why: Provides a human-readable resonant-peak-shape reference for the HP stage across resonance settings, useful for spotting a shape regression by eye even though nothing here is asserted numerically.
entries.deviationMeans: This test cannot fail meaningfully (expect(true)) — if the HP stage's resonant shape has regressed, it would only be visible by reading the printed table, not from a red test.

entries.key: OverdriveDiagnostic / HuggettFilter separation: LP response vs sep (db12 vs db24)
entries.what: Diagnostic: prints LP response tables across 6 probe frequencies and 5 separation values {-2,-1,0,1,2} for both 12dB and 24dB slopes; asserts only expect(true) — a reporting tool, not a gate.
entries.why: Human-readable reference table for how separation reshapes the LP response at each slope — complements the numerically-gated HuggettSeparationTests by making the actual shape visible for by-eye inspection.
entries.deviationMeans: This test cannot fail meaningfully — a separation-shape regression would only be caught by the gated HuggettSeparationTests, not by this diagnostic printout.

entries.key: FilterModelLibrary / entry 0 is Huggett and is stable
entries.what: Asserts FilterModelLibrary::count() >= 1 and id(0) == "huggett".
entries.why: ADR-0011 declares Huggett the default, stable-ID entry 0 of the append-only FilterModel registry; index 0 being Huggett is load-bearing for anything that defaults to index 0 without an explicit selection.
entries.deviationMeans: Huggett is no longer registered at index 0 — any code (including saved presets, per Q16's migration shim) relying on 'default filter = index 0' would silently select a different model.

entries.key: FilterModelLibrary / names() count matches and is non-empty
entries.what: Asserts FilterModelLibrary::names().size() equals count(), and names()[0] is non-empty.
entries.why: The UI's spine.filterModel selector is built from names(); a count mismatch would mean the selector shows the wrong number of choices relative to what create()/id() can actually produce.
entries.deviationMeans: The filter-model name list and the actual registered-model count have desynced — the UI selector could show too few/many choices, or an empty label for a real model.

entries.key: FilterModelLibrary / create() returns a usable model
entries.what: Asserts FilterModelLibrary::create(0) returns a non-null model instance.
entries.why: create() is the factory every voice slot calls to instantiate a FilterModel by registry index; a null return here would mean the most basic registry operation is broken.
entries.deviationMeans: The filter-model factory is failing to construct a model for a valid index — any voice trying to bind to that model would get a null model, likely crashing or silently passing audio unfiltered.

entries.key: FilterModelLibrary / Moog is registered as the second model
entries.what: Asserts count()==2, names() contains "Moog", id(1)=="moog", and create(1) dynamic_casts successfully to MoogLadder*.
entries.why: Pins Moog's registration as ADR-0011's second library entry (shipped v5.2, per followup_moog_filter_consolidation) — confirms both the metadata (id/name) and the factory (create returns an actual MoogLadder, not some other type) are correctly wired for the second model.
entries.deviationMeans: Moog's registry slot is broken — either the id/name metadata is wrong, or create(1) is returning a different (or null) model type, meaning selecting 'Moog' in the UI wouldn't actually instantiate a MoogLadder.

entries.key: InPlaceState / constructState (in-place) matches makeState (heap) sample-for-sample
entries.what: Renders a 200 Hz tone through the same HuggettFilter config using both the heap-allocated makeState() path and the in-place constructState(buf) path into a stack buffer; asserts the two output buffers are bit-identical via memcmp.
entries.why: L7's heap-free, in-place per-voice model-slot design (the live-switch crossfade architecture) requires the in-place construction path to be functionally identical to the heap path it replaces — this is the foundational proof for the whole hot-swap architecture.
entries.deviationMeans: The in-place state construction produces different output than heap allocation for the same filter config — the entire hot-swap/crossfade architecture (Q17) would be built on a broken equivalence, meaning live filter switching could sound subtly different from a freshly-allocated filter.

entries.key: InPlaceState / every registered model fits kMaxSpineStateBytes (Q18)
entries.what: Iterates every model in FilterModelLibrary and asserts each one's stateSize() <= kMaxSpineStateBytes (512) and stateAlign() <= kSpineStateAlign.
entries.why: This is Q18's resolved governance mechanism made runtime-checkable: the two in-place model slots are sized to the largest FilterModel, and Q18 specifies a compile-time static_assert PLUS this runtime check as the guard that keeps L7's heap-free invariant intact as models are appended.
entries.deviationMeans: A registered filter model's state no longer fits the shared per-voice slot budget — appending or growing a model exceeded the 512-byte cap without bumping kMaxSpineStateBytes (per Q18, the fix is to bump the constant or slim the model, never silently overflow).

entries.key: InPlaceState / CountingFilterModel: construct/destroy balance
entries.what: Using a test-only CountingFilterModel fixture, asserts constructState() increments a live-instance counter by exactly 1 and destroyState() decrements it back to the prior value.
entries.why: Directly targets the P0 heap-use-after-free class of bug (per Layer's 're-prepare' test in LayerTests.cpp, found by ASan via the OS-change lifecycle path) — an unbalanced construct/destroy would mean states are leaking or being double-freed.
entries.deviationMeans: Filter-model state construction and destruction are unbalanced — this is exactly the kind of lifecycle bug that produced the P0 heap-use-after-free; treat any regression here as a potential memory-safety issue, not a cosmetic counter mismatch.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / steady state outputs the active model (gain 1.0)
entries.what: Processes a steady 0.5 tone through a SpineFilterSlot bound to model A (gain 1.0) and asserts the settled output is within 1e-3 of 0.5 (unchanged) — no fade in progress.
entries.why: Baseline steady-state correctness before any crossfade behavior is tested — the SpineFilterSlot must pass audio through the active model unmodified when nothing is switching.
entries.deviationMeans: The hot-swap slot's steady-state passthrough (no fade active) is altering the signal — a regression that would affect every voice all the time, not just during a model switch.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / click-free switch: no large sample-to-sample jump across the change
entries.what: Switches from model A to model B (25 ms fade) and asserts the max sample-to-sample jump anywhere in the first block after the switch is under 0.05 (no discontinuity).
entries.why: Q17's click-free live-switch guarantee (the whole reason for the equal-power crossfade design) — a live spine.filterModel change during performance must never produce an audible click.
entries.deviationMeans: Switching filter models live is producing an audible click — Q17's core guarantee (the reason the crossfade architecture exists at all) has regressed.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / fade completes toward model B (gain 0.25) within ~fadeLen
entries.what: Runs ~1400 samples (>fadeLen=1200 for a 25ms fade @48k) after switching to model B (gain 0.25) and asserts the output has fully settled to 0.5*0.25=0.125 within 5e-3.
entries.why: Confirms the crossfade actually COMPLETES within its configured duration — a fade that never finishes (or overshoots/undershoots the target) would leave the voice stuck at an intermediate blend indefinitely.
entries.deviationMeans: The crossfade is not completing within its configured duration — a filter-model switch could leave the voice audibly 'stuck between' two models long after the fade should have finished.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / no leak: live states return to baseline+1 (one active buffer)
entries.what: After the fade to model B completes, asserts CountingFilterModel::liveStates() equals its pre-test baseline + 1 (exactly one active state remains, the old model's state was freed).
entries.why: The crossfade must free the OLD model's in-place state once the fade completes and it's no longer needed — leaking it would silently grow per-voice memory with every model switch, violating L7's heap-free-at-steady-state design.
entries.deviationMeans: A completed crossfade is leaking the old model's state instead of freeing it — every live filter-model switch during a session would accumulate leaked state, eventually threatening the kMaxSpineStateBytes budget (Q18) or just wasting memory.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / coalesce depth-1: A->B then mid-fade ->A settles back to A
entries.what: Starts an A->B fade, then mid-fade re-targets back to A; runs enough samples for the coalesced fade to settle, and asserts the output returns fully to model A's steady state (0.5).
entries.why: Q17's resolved 'coalesce depth-1' policy (at most one fade in flight; newest pending wins) — this proves a rapid double-switch (e.g. user flicking the selector quickly) resolves cleanly to the LAST requested model, not some stuck intermediate blend.
entries.deviationMeans: Rapidly re-selecting a filter model mid-fade no longer resolves correctly to the final choice — the coalesce-depth-1 policy from Q17 has regressed, risking a voice stuck between two models if the selector is stepped quickly.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / bind() snaps to a different model with no fade (Q17b unit)
entries.what: Calls bind() to snap a fresh slot directly onto model B (simulating a stolen voice starting a new note on a different layer's model) and asserts the very next block already reads model B's exact steady state (0.125) with no fade transition.
entries.why: This is Q17b's closed cross-layer voice-steal fix: note-start snap-bind (SpineFilterSlot::bind) reconstructs in-place state for the new layer's model with NO fade — a freshly-triggered note must sound like its own layer's model immediately, not fade in from whatever the stolen voice was previously playing.
entries.deviationMeans: A stolen voice starting a new note on a different layer is fading in from the old layer's model instead of snapping immediately — this is the Q17b cross-layer voice-steal bug returning (a new note briefly sounding like the wrong filter model).

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / bind() cancels an in-flight fade and leaks no state
entries.what: Starts an A->B fade, then calls bind() back to A mid-fade (simulating voice-steal during an in-flight crossfade); asserts liveStates() returns to baseline+1 (the B state was freed, not leaked, by the bind-triggered cancellation).
entries.why: Combines Q17 (fade cancellation) and Q18 (heap-free invariant) concerns — voice-stealing a voice that happens to be mid-crossfade must not leak the fade's second (fading-in) model state.
entries.deviationMeans: Voice-stealing during an in-flight crossfade is leaking the fading-in model's state — repeated steals during fades would accumulate leaked memory over a session.

entries.key: ModelHotSwap / live crossfade Huggett <-> Moog stays finite and bounded
entries.what: Renders a real voice (saw, cutoff 800 Hz, resonance 0.1/Q~1.0) through a full Layer/Voice, switches spineModel from Huggett to Moog mid-note, and tracks peak magnitude across the crossfade+settle window (8 blocks of 512 samples); asserts output stays finite and the crossfade-window peak stays under 4.0.
entries.why: The full end-to-end, real-filter-models version of the click-free/bounded hot-swap guarantee — proves the crossfade behaves safely when the two models are actually Huggett and Moog (very different DSP, including Huggett's Q27-relevant resonant nonlinearity), not just the CountingFilterModel test double.
entries.deviationMeans: Live-switching between the two real, shipping filter models mid-note is producing non-finite or unbounded output — given Huggett's resonance-loop history (Q27), treat any blow-up here as a potential interaction between the hot-swap crossfade and Huggett's nonlinear state, not just a generic hot-swap bug.

entries.key: Layer / Layer prepares and a Voice renders through it
entries.what: Builds a Layer with a sine oscillator (cutoff 20kHz effectively bypassing the filter) and a fast envelope, drives a Voice through it with noteOn(69, A4), and asserts the rendered 256-sample buffer's total energy exceeds 1e-4 (audibly non-silent).
entries.why: The most basic end-to-end smoke test of the Layer->Voice audio path — every more specific Layer test below assumes this baseline signal chain (osc->envelope->spine->output) actually produces audio at all.
entries.deviationMeans: The fundamental Layer-driven voice rendering path is broken — a note-on through a Layer produces silence, which would manifest as no sound from the synth at all regardless of any specific parameter.

entries.key: Layer / Layer exposes a configured HP stage
entries.what: Configures a Layer's HP pre-filter (hpCutoffHz=2000, slope=1) and asserts layer.hpStage() is non-null, then filters a 200 Hz tone through the exposed stage's own state and asserts the peak is attenuated below 0.6.
entries.why: The HP pre-filter is a distinct v5.0-shipped stage the Layer must expose for external configuration/probing (used elsewhere by MultiLayerTests' HP test) — this proves the Layer wiring exposes a genuinely-configured, working HP stage, not a stub.
entries.deviationMeans: The Layer's HP stage accessor is returning null or an unconfigured/non-attenuating stage — code that probes or reconfigures the HP stage through the Layer (rather than through full audio rendering) would be broken.

entries.key: Layer / pre-built models: spineModel(id) is stable across updateParameters
entries.what: Captures the FilterModel* returned by layer.spineModel(0) before and after an updateParameters() call selecting model 0; asserts the pointer is identical (models are pre-built once, not rebuilt on every parameter update) and that spineModel() (current) equals spineModel(0).
entries.why: Performance/correctness invariant: models must be constructed once at prepare() time and merely SELECTED (not recreated) by updateParameters, since recreating them on every parameter tweak would be needlessly expensive and would invalidate any code holding a pointer to the previous instance.
entries.deviationMeans: updateParameters is rebuilding filter model instances on every call instead of reusing pre-built ones — besides wasted CPU, this is exactly the class of bug the next test (re-prepare stability) exists to catch when it happens on re-PREPARE instead of update.

entries.key: Layer / model instances are stable across re-prepare (voice slots cache FilterModel*)
entries.what: Captures spineModel(0) and spineModel(1) pointers, calls layer.prepare() again with a different sample rate/block size (simulating an OS-factor change), and asserts both pointers are unchanged.
entries.why: Direct regression test for a P0 heap-use-after-free bug the code comment documents explicitly: SpineFilterSlot caches a non-owning FilterModel* to destroy its in-place per-voice state, and if Layer::prepare recreates the models_ array, every cached pointer dangles — this was found by ASan via the OS-change lifecycle path.
entries.deviationMeans: Re-preparing a Layer (e.g. on an oversampling-factor change) is recreating its FilterModel instances — every voice slot's cached FilterModel* now dangles, which is a use-after-free waiting to happen the next time a voice tries to destroy its state; this is the exact P0 bug class, treat as critical.

entries.key: Layer / Layer routes Moog params to the Moog instance and processes through it
entries.what: Selects spineModel=1 (Moog) via ParamSnapshot, asserts the active model dynamic_casts to MoogLadder*, then compares 100 Hz tone RMS through Moog LP mode vs HP mode (cutoff 1000 Hz) — asserts LP passes (rms>1e-4) and HP attenuates to less than half LP's RMS, proving mode dispatch (not just model selection) reaches the real MoogLadder.
entries.why: Guards against a dispatch-removal regression noted in the code: 'If the dispatch block is removed, setMode() is never called and both runs produce identical output' — proving Layer forwards mode changes all the way to the concrete Moog instance, not just the model-selection index.
entries.deviationMeans: Selecting Moog's LP vs HP mode through the Layer no longer changes its filtering behavior — the parameter-routing dispatch to the concrete MoogLadder's setMode() has been silently dropped, even though model SELECTION (Huggett vs Moog) still works.

entries.key: Layer / Lowering the cutoff drops high-note energy
entries.what: Renders the same high note (MIDI 108) through a Layer at cutoff=100 Hz vs cutoff=20000 Hz; asserts the high-cutoff render has more than 2x the energy of the low-cutoff render.
entries.why: Coarse end-to-end sanity that the spine filter's cutoff parameter, reached through the full Layer/Voice signal chain (not a bare filter unit test), audibly gates high-frequency content as expected.
entries.deviationMeans: The cutoff parameter has stopped meaningfully shaping voice output through the full signal chain — even if the underlying filter unit tests pass in isolation, something in the Layer/Voice parameter wiring has broken the end-to-end effect.

entries.key: AlgorithmRouting / shaper: waveshaper drive changes the output (the shaper block is in the graph)
entries.what: Renders a full voice with the 'shaper' algorithm at wsDrive=0 vs wsDrive=1 and asserts the summed absolute per-sample difference exceeds 1e-3, proving the Waveshaper block is actually wired into that algorithm's signal path.
entries.why: AlgorithmLibrary is data (a slot/block-type table); this test proves the data is not inert — the graph-execution code actually instantiates and processes through the declared block for the 'shaper' algorithm.
entries.deviationMeans: The waveshaper drive parameter no longer audibly affects the 'shaper' algorithm's output — the algorithm-to-block-instantiation wiring is broken, even though the library metadata may look correct.

entries.key: AlgorithmRouting / thru: waveshaper drive has no effect (no shaper block in the graph)
entries.what: Same drive-sweep render as the shaper case but on the empty 'thru' algorithm; asserts the summed difference is within 1e-5 of zero (drive parameter has no block to act on).
entries.why: The negative-control counterpart to the shaper-routing test — proves silently-unused parameters don't leak into algorithms that don't declare the corresponding block, which would mean the graph ignores its own topology.
entries.deviationMeans: The 'thru' (empty) algorithm is picking up waveshaper drive despite declaring zero slots — the algorithm graph is not actually gating processing by the declared block list.

entries.key: LayerRouting / disabled layer never matches
entries.what: With enable=false, asserts matches(60, 100, 1) returns false regardless of key/velocity/channel.
entries.why: The enable flag is the master gate for a Layer's MIDI routing (used by MultiLayerTests to prove a disabled layer produces zero voices) — it must override every other routing criterion unconditionally.
entries.deviationMeans: A disabled layer is still matching MIDI input — notes could sound on a layer the user explicitly turned off.

entries.key: LayerRouting / full-range enabled layer matches anything on its channel
entries.what: With enable=true, full key/velocity range (0-127/1-127), and channel=0 (Omni), asserts both matches(0,1,1) and matches(127,127,16) succeed.
entries.why: Baseline positive-path check that the default/full-range configuration (the common single-layer case) matches broadly as expected, before testing the narrowing behavior of key/velocity/channel gates individually.
entries.deviationMeans: A layer configured for full-range Omni matching is rejecting valid MIDI input — the most common single-layer patch configuration would silently produce no sound.

entries.key: LayerRouting / key range gates (split)
entries.what: With keyLo=60/keyHi=127, asserts matches(59,...) fails and matches(60,...) succeeds — the exact boundary key.
entries.why: Key-range gating is the mechanism behind keyboard splits (MultiLayerTests' split test relies on this); the boundary must be inclusive at keyLo, exclusive just below it.
entries.deviationMeans: A keyboard split's boundary key is off by one or the range check is broken entirely — notes at or near a split point would route to the wrong layer (or no layer).

entries.key: LayerRouting / velocity range gates
entries.what: With velLo=64/velHi=127, asserts matches(60,63,...) fails and matches(60,64,...) succeeds — the exact boundary velocity.
entries.why: Velocity-range gating enables velocity-switched layering (e.g. soft vs hard layer articulations); the boundary correctness matters for predictable velocity-switch points.
entries.deviationMeans: A velocity-switched patch's boundary velocity is off by one or velocity gating is broken — layers would switch at the wrong dynamic level or not at all.

entries.key: LayerRouting / channel filter: specific channel only matches that channel
entries.what: With channel=2, asserts matches(60,100,2) succeeds and matches(60,100,1) fails.
entries.why: MIDI-channel filtering lets multiple Layers be addressed independently (e.g. multitimbral setups) — a specific-channel layer must not respond to other channels.
entries.deviationMeans: Channel-specific layer routing is leaking across channels — a layer bound to one MIDI channel would incorrectly respond to notes on another.

entries.key: MultiLayer / split: a note fires only the layer whose key range contains it
entries.what: Configures layer0 (audible) for keys 0-59 and layer1 (silenced via level=0) for keys 60-127; asserts a low note (48) produces energy and a high note (70) produces exactly zero energy (within 1e-9).
entries.why: Proves keyboard-split routing end-to-end through the full Program/VoiceManager stack — the silent-upper-layer trick specifically distinguishes 'correctly routed to the right (silent) layer' from 'accidentally still audible via the wrong layer'.
entries.deviationMeans: Keyboard split routing is broken at the Program/VoiceManager level — notes could sound on the wrong layer or bleed across the split point, even if LayerRoutingTests' lower-level matches() logic passes in isolation.

entries.key: MultiLayer / stacking: a note matching both layers fires both (more energy)
entries.what: Configures both layers to match the same note (69); compares total output energy with both layers enabled vs only one, asserting stacked energy exceeds 2x single-layer energy (two phase-coherent voices ~2x amplitude ~4x energy).
entries.why: Proves layer-stacking (multiple layers responding to one note, e.g. a split+stack patch) actually renders multiple simultaneous voices rather than one layer silently winning.
entries.deviationMeans: Layer stacking is not producing additional voices — a patch designed to stack two layers on one note would sound like only one layer is present.

entries.key: MultiLayer / layer level scales contribution
entries.what: Renders the same note at layer level 1.0 vs 0.25; asserts the higher-level render has more than 2x the energy of the lower-level render.
entries.why: The per-layer level parameter (used for balancing stacked/split layers) must actually scale each layer's audio contribution, not just be a cosmetic UI value.
entries.deviationMeans: Adjusting a layer's level fader no longer meaningfully changes its audio contribution — layer balance mixing would be broken.

entries.key: MultiLayer / disabled layer produces no voices
entries.what: With both layer routings disabled, renders a note and asserts output energy is exactly zero (within 1e-9).
entries.why: A fully-disabled program (both layers off) must produce true silence, not some residual voice leak — the negative-control baseline for all the other MultiLayer routing tests.
entries.deviationMeans: A program with no enabled layers is still producing audio — voices are being created despite routing saying no layer should match, a fundamental voice-allocation bug.

entries.key: MultiLayer / a note on layer 1 uses layer 1's spine filter, not layer 0's
