entries.why: M15 - proves the filter's decaying resonance tail eventually flushes to true digital silence (denormal-safe) rather than hanging at a tiny non-zero level indefinitely, which would waste CPU on denormal-slow floating point or leave an inaudible-but-technically-nonzero DC-adjacent tail.
entries.deviationMeans: The filter's decay tail is not reaching true silence within 1 second - could indicate a denormal-handling regression (CPU-expensive denormal floats) or a subtle state leak that prevents full decay.

entries.key: SpineNlSvfHarness / SpineNlSvf M9 zipper NSR <= static baseline + 3 dB
entries.what: Holds a bin-aligned 200 Hz tone while ramping cutoff 400->4000 Hz over 128-sample blocks (2s), measures inharmonic energy (zipper noise) via NSR, and gates it at <= -12.0 dB — an explicitly-documented REGRESSION anchor (measured -15.06 dB baseline + 3 dB headroom), not a 'zipper-free' claim, since the cell has no cutoff smoothing yet.
entries.why: M9 — the code comment is explicit that this catches a WORSENING of a KNOWN, accepted zipper artifact (the v5 per-parameter smoothing sub-step is unbuilt), pending either cutoff smoothing landing (which would let the gate tighten toward the static baseline) or a calibration decision about whether -15 dB matches real synths.
entries.deviationMeans: Per-block cutoff automation is producing MORE zipper noise than the already-accepted baseline — since -15 dB zipper is a known, accepted-for-now limitation (not itself a bug), a regression here means it got WORSE, not that zipper exists at all.

entries.key: SpineDcBlockerHarness / SpineDcBlocker M14a DC removed (tail |mean| <= 0.02)
entries.what: Feeds 0.5 DC + 200 Hz sine through DcBlocker for 8192 samples and gates the tail (samples 6000-8192) mean absolute value at <= 0.02 via testdsp::Gate.
entries.why: M14a in the acceptance-criterion metric numbering — a gated, machine-readable fixture-harness port of the same DC-removal property HuggettNonlinearTests checks with a plain expect(), now wired through the Gate/printf reporting convention used by the deeper characterization fixtures.
entries.deviationMeans: DC removal has regressed against the formal M14a gate threshold — since this is a port/duplicate of HuggettNonlinearTests' DcBlocker check, compare both to see if the regression is DcBlocker itself or specific to this harness's gate wiring.

entries.key: SpineDcBlockerHarness / SpineDcBlocker M14b audio RMS preserved (>= 0.5x input)
entries.what: On the same 0.5-DC+200Hz-sine signal, compares tail-window RMS of output vs input and gates output RMS at >= 0.5x input RMS.
entries.why: M14b — proves DC removal isn't achieved by also attenuating the legitimate AC audio content riding on the DC offset (a naive/aggressive DC blocker could remove both).
entries.deviationMeans: The DC blocker is over-attenuating audio content along with the DC offset — every asymmetric-saturator-driven signal passing through a DcBlocker downstream would lose audible level, not just DC.

entries.key: SpineDcBlockerHarness / SpineDcBlocker M14c L/R independence (opposite DC, both -> 0)
entries.what: Feeds +0.5 DC to channel 0 and -0.5 DC to channel 1 of the same DcBlocker instance; gates both channels' final sample at <= 0.05 absolute value (both converge toward zero independently).
entries.why: M14c, an explicit port of HuggettNonlinearTests' 'DcBlocker keeps L/R state independent' check (per the file's header comment) into the gated fixture-harness convention — a state-sharing bug would let opposite-sign DC cross-contaminate and at least one channel would fail to converge.
entries.deviationMeans: The DC blocker's L/R state has become coupled — identical regression concern to the original HuggettNonlinearTests version; check both to see if the bug is in DcBlocker itself or specific to this harness wiring.

entries.key: SpineHpStageHarness / SpineHpStage M10 HP corner: highs pass, lows cut (12 vs 24)
entries.what: Gates HuggettHpStage at fc=1000: highs (8kHz, db12) pass >= 0.7, lows (200Hz, db12) cut <= 0.1, 24dB is steeper than 12dB below the corner, and 24dB strongly cuts lows (<=0.04) — a gated port of HpPreFilterTests' plain-expect version of the same properties.
entries.why: M10 in the acceptance-criterion numbering — the gated-harness counterpart to HpPreFilterTests, using the same measurement helper pattern (peakMag) explicitly noted in the code comment as matching HpPreFilterTests' 'mag' lambda exactly.
entries.deviationMeans: The HP stage's basic corner/slope behavior has regressed against the formal M10 gate — cross-check against HpPreFilterTests (the original, non-gated version) to see if both fail together (a real DSP regression) or just this harness (a gate-wiring issue).

entries.key: SpineHpStageHarness / SpineHpStage M11 resonant peak above HP corner
entries.what: At res=0.5 (small-signal, linear regime), scans 25 points from 900-1400 Hz around a 1000 Hz HP corner, finds the peak magnitude, and gates its frequency within 2% of fc and its height at least 1 dB above a passband reference (measured at 8000 Hz) — a resonant bump analytically expected from the 2-pole HP transfer function H_hp = u^2/sqrt((1-u^2)^2+k^2*u^2).
entries.why: M11 — this is a HP-stage-specific resonant-peak test with no direct HpPreFilterTests counterpart (a genuinely new fixture-harness measurement), verifying the HP stage's resonance behaves per the documented analytic HP peak formula, not just that it self-oscillates safely at max resonance.
entries.deviationMeans: The HP stage's resonant peak (at moderate, non-extreme resonance) is missing, mistuned, or too subtle — the HP stage's Q/resonance behavior in its normal playing range (not just the max-resonance self-osc edge case) has regressed.

entries.key: SpineHpStageHarness / SpineHpStage M2 self-osc bounded <= 4.0 (max res, db24)
entries.what: Kicks HuggettHpStage at fc=1200/res=1.0/24dB with an impulse, runs 24000 samples, and gates the settled peak at <= 4.0 with no NaN/Inf — a gated port of HpPreFilterTests' self-oscillation-bounded check.
entries.why: M2 in the acceptance-criterion numbering — the same Q27-adjacent bounded-resonance safety property (state-blow-up prevention) as HpPreFilterTests, now wired through the formal Gate/M-number convention used by the deeper fixture harnesses.
entries.deviationMeans: The HP stage's max-resonance self-oscillation is unbounded against the formal M2 gate — cross-check against HpPreFilterTests to see if the underlying DSP regressed (both fail) or only this harness's gate wiring did.

entries.key: FilterUnderTest / Moog FUT: supports LP/BP/HP, rejects Notch
entries.what: Asserts the Moog FUT reports supports(LP24)==true, supports(BP)==true, supports(Notch)==false, and name()=="moog".
entries.why: Mode-support declarations gate which measurement batteries CharacterizationRunner attempts against a model — Moog genuinely has no Notch mode (unlike Huggett's nine-routing set, Q14), so the harness must not try to measure a mode the model can't produce.
entries.deviationMeans: Moog's declared mode support no longer matches its real capabilities — either the runner will try to measure a nonexistent Notch response (garbage data) or skip a mode Moog actually now supports.

entries.key: FilterUnderTest / Huggett FUT: supports Notch
entries.what: Asserts the Huggett FUT reports supports(Notch)==true and name()=="huggett".
entries.why: Huggett's Notch mode is part of the nine-routing dual-filter set shipped in Q14/v5.2; this is the measurement-harness-side declaration that lets CharacterizationRunnerTests' Notch battery run for Huggett specifically.
entries.deviationMeans: Huggett's Notch mode is no longer declared as supported — the characterization runner would skip measuring it, silently dropping coverage of one of the nine shipped routings.

entries.key: FilterUnderTest / Moog LP24 passes bass, rejects treble (socket drives a real measurement)
entries.what: Sets Moog to LP24 fc=1000 Hz via the OperatingPoint socket and measures 100 Hz and 8000 Hz; asserts 100 Hz reads above -3 dB (near passband) and 8000 Hz reads below -24 dB (strongly attenuated).
entries.why: End-to-end proof that the FilterUnderTest's OperatingPoint parameterization socket actually drives a real, correctly-shaped Moog measurement — not just that the interface compiles, but that setting an operating point produces the expected filter behavior.
entries.deviationMeans: Setting an OperatingPoint on the Moog FUT no longer produces the expected LP shape — either the socket isn't correctly forwarding to the real MoogLadder, or the ladder itself has a basic LP regression.

entries.key: FilterUnderTest / OS factors are in-band transparent (LP, probe well below cutoff)
entries.what: Measures Moog LP24 fc=2000 Hz at 200 Hz across osFactor {1,2,4,8} and asserts each oversampled measurement stays within 0.5 dB of the os1 baseline.
entries.why: In-band content well below cutoff should be essentially unaffected by the OS factor chosen (Q12's OS policy) — this is the harness-level guard that oversampling changes cost/aliasing behavior, not the passband gain of unrelated in-band content.
entries.deviationMeans: Changing the oversampling factor is altering in-band gain at a frequency nowhere near the filter's transition — the OS-path implementation (Halfband2x, Q23) has a gain/level bug independent of its aliasing performance.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / coarseGrid is bounded: 96k only, OS {1,2,4,8}, live only
entries.what: Asserts chz::coarseGrid() is fixed to a single 96 kHz host rate, all four OS factors {1,2,4,8}, and Live-only osMode (no Render mode).
entries.why: coarseGrid() is the 10-25 minute full characterization sweep; pinning its shape prevents an accidental grid explosion (e.g. adding host rates or Render mode) from silently multiplying runtime beyond the documented budget — directly relevant to the Q28 grid-economy problem (36k-point fullGrid pricing).
entries.deviationMeans: The coarse characterization grid's dimensions have changed — check the actual runtime against the documented 10-25 min budget; an expansion here compounds with Q28's already-expensive fullGrid.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / runner produces LP24 headline metrics for Moog and writes a CSV
entries.what: Runs a tiny 1-point Moog LP24 fc=1000 grid and asserts corner_hz lands in 200-2000 Hz (the authentic 4-pole cascade corner sits at ~0.44*fc, not fc, per the code comment) and slope_db_oct < -3.0, plus that response.csv is written to the output dir.
entries.why: Documents and pins the hardware-accurate detail that a 4-pole Moog cascade's -3 dB point is NOT at the nominal cutoff but at sqrt(2^0.25-1)*fc ≈ 0.435*fc — a naive +-1-octave sanity window would wrongly flag correct behavior as a bug, hence the widened +2/-2.3 octave window.
entries.deviationMeans: Either the corner has moved outside the accepted hardware-accurate range (real regression) or the window itself needs adjusting — check against the FilterUnderTestTests stopband-attenuation test before concluding either way.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / B2+B3: resonance.csv + distortion.csv exist; B2/B3 summary keys present and finite
entries.what: Runs Moog LP24 at res={0,0.9} and osFactors={1,2}; asserts resonance.csv and distortion.csv are written and that selfosc_cents_err, thd_db, alias_db@os1, alias_db@os2 summary keys are each either a plausible finite value (> -900) or exactly the -1.0 not-detected sentinel.
entries.why: Fixes a documented bug ("FIX 5") where isfinite(-1.0) is trivially true, making the old finite-OR-sentinel check dead code that would have let an absurd value like -5756 cents pass silently; this test pins the corrected plausible-range-or-exact-sentinel logic.
entries.deviationMeans: A B2 (self-osc pitch) or B3 (THD/aliasing) battery metric is producing an implausible value that isn't the sentinel — the runner's self-osc detection or THD/alias measurement has regressed.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / Huggett runs the same battery (model-agnostic socket) incl. Notch
entries.what: Runs the Huggett FUT through LP24 and Notch modes and asserts both produce a corner_hz summary key and response.csv is written; values are logged, not tightly bounded (this test is about model-agnosticism, not Huggett voicing).
entries.why: Proves the CharacterizationRunner socket (FilterUnderTest interface) is genuinely model-agnostic — the same runner code drives both Moog and Huggett, including Huggett's extra Notch mode (part of the nine-routing set from Q14), without per-model special-casing.
entries.deviationMeans: The runner no longer produces results for Huggett or its Notch mode — the FilterUnderTest abstraction has broken for a specific model or mode, undermining the 'one harness for every filter model' design.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / aliasing decreases as OS factor rises (Moog, driven LP)
entries.what: Runs a driven (drive=1.0) Moog LP24 fc=4000 across osFactors {1,2,4,8} and asserts alias_db at os8 is more than 3 dB lower than at os1.
entries.why: The whole point of oversampling the drive stages (Q12's ADAA/OS policy) is to reduce audible aliasing from the nonlinearity; this is the end-to-end proof that raising OS factor actually buys a measurable aliasing reduction, not just added CPU cost.
entries.deviationMeans: Higher oversampling factors are no longer reducing aliasing on a driven filter — the OS/anti-aliasing pipeline (Q12, or the Q23 halfband optimization) may have a correctness regression that makes the extra CPU cost pointless.

entries.key: CharacterizationRunner / Moog HP fc4000: methods agree in-band (deep stopband is noise-limited)
entries.what: Measures Moog HP fc=4000 via both stepped-sine and ESS across 200 probes 20 Hz-24 kHz; asserts the IN-BAND (within 40 dB of passband peak) method delta is < 1.0 dB, while documenting that the UNSCOPED delta (including the ~-60 dB+ deep stopband where both methods hit their noise floor and scatter ~5 dB) would be a meaningless ~90 dB.
entries.why: Regression test for a method-agreement SCOPING bug — an HP filter's deep stopband is where measurement noise dominates the signal, so comparing methods there measures noise-floor scatter, not filter (dis)agreement; this pins the in-band-only comparison as the meaningful metric.
entries.deviationMeans: The in-band method-agreement window itself has broken, or the passband-relative windowing logic regressed — if this starts failing, check whether the 40 dB in-band definition still correctly excludes the noise-limited stopband.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / moog: LP24 fc1000 slope_db_oct <= -3.0 (rolling off)
entries.what: Runs a fast always-on gate grid (LP24, fc=1000 Hz, res 0/0.9, os1, hostSR 96k, 40 probes) on the Moog FUT and asserts slope_db_oct <= -3.0 dB/oct, confirming the filter genuinely rolls off above its corner.
entries.why: This is the CI-fast (~5s added) spec gate distinguishing a working LP filter from a broken/flat one, run on every build — the coarse full characterization grid (10-25 min) is too slow to gate on.
entries.deviationMeans: The Moog LP24 filter is no longer rolling off above its cutoff at a basic sanity threshold — a fundamental filter-shape regression, not a subtle voicing change.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / moog: LP24 fc1000 method_delta_db <= 1.0 (in-band dual-method agreement)
entries.what: Asserts the in-band (within 40 dB of passband peak) max magnitude delta between the stepped-sine and ESS measurement methods stays <= 1.0 dB for Moog LP24 fc=1000 in the gate grid (measured ~0.31 dB).
entries.why: The dual-method agreement gate (filter-validation harness's core trust mechanism) proves the measured response isn't a measurement-method artifact; if the two independent methods disagree, neither can be trusted as ground truth.
entries.deviationMeans: Stepped-sine and ESS measurements of the same filter now disagree beyond gate tolerance — either the filter's response has become method-sensitive (e.g. time-varying/nonlinear in a way that breaks LTI assumptions) or one of the measurement methods has a bug.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / moog: self-golden (LP24/fc1000 headline metrics)
entries.what: Compares Moog's corner_hz, slope_db_oct, method_delta_db, selfosc_cents_err, peak_gain_db, passband_gain_db, noise_floor_dbfs and noise_floor_dbfsA against committed golden baselines (tests/golden/moog/baseline.csv) with per-metric tolerances.
entries.why: Self-golden regression catches ANY drift in Moog's headline characterization numbers between commits, even changes too subtle for the coarse spec gates above to catch — golden updates require BERNIE_UPDATE_GOLDEN=1 and a justified commit message per the build rails.
entries.deviationMeans: One of Moog's headline metrics moved outside its golden tolerance — investigate whether this is an intentional voicing/DSP change (requires a golden regen + commit justification) or an unintended regression.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / huggett: LP24 fc1000 slope_db_oct <= -3.0 (rolling off)
entries.what: Same gate-grid slope sanity check as the Moog case but for Huggett LP24 fc=1000 — asserts slope_db_oct <= -3.0 dB/oct.
entries.why: Mirrors the Moog gate for the other live filter model; both models must pass the same fast CI sanity check since either can be selected live via spine.filterModel (ADR-0011).
entries.deviationMeans: Huggett LP24 is no longer rolling off at a basic sanity threshold — check for a regression in the dual-SVF-cell topology (Q13) or the Q27 bounded-resonance fix's interaction with the base LP response.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / huggett: LP24 fc1000 method_delta_db <= 1.0 (in-band dual-method agreement)
entries.what: Same in-band stepped-sine-vs-ESS agreement check as Moog's, applied to Huggett LP24 fc=1000, asserting <= 1.0 dB delta.
entries.why: Ensures the dual-method trust guarantee holds for Huggett too, including after the Q27 resonance-loop fix which changed its nonlinear behavior at high resonance.
entries.deviationMeans: Huggett's two measurement methods disagree beyond tolerance — could indicate the Q27 fix (or any subsequent nonlinear change) introduced time-variance or instability that breaks the LTI measurement assumption.

entries.key: CharacterizationGate / huggett: self-golden (LP24/fc1000 headline metrics)
entries.what: Compares Huggett's headline metrics (corner_hz, slope_db_oct, method_delta_db, peak_gain_db, passband_gain_db, noise floors — self-osc golden skipped, includeSelfOsc=false) against tests/golden/huggett/baseline.csv.
entries.why: Huggett's self-osc golden is deliberately excluded here (unlike Moog) because Huggett doesn't reliably self-oscillate at this gate grid's resonance; the peak_gain_db check is explicitly noted as a coarse-grid regression PROXY, not the true peak (true peak/disparity is LevelDisparityTests' job).
entries.deviationMeans: A Huggett headline metric drifted from its golden baseline — given the Q27 history (resonance loop defect/fix) treat any peak_gain_db or slope drift with extra scrutiny before accepting a golden regen.

entries.key: DeviceUnderTest / descriptor reports kind / excitation / name
entries.what: Verifies a minimal PassthroughDevice implementing DeviceUnderTest reports kind()==TransferFunction, excitation()==InputSweep, and name()=="passthrough" through the abstract base.
entries.why: DeviceUnderTest is the harness-wide polymorphic contract every measured thing (real filters, reference devices, calibration tones) implements; this is the minimal-implementation proof that the contract's descriptor methods work with nothing else present.
entries.deviationMeans: The DeviceUnderTest base interface's descriptor methods are broken for even a trivial implementation — every concrete device built on this abstraction is suspect.

entries.key: DeviceUnderTest / L0 ruler measures a device through the base (unity ~0 dB)
entries.what: Measures the unity-gain PassthroughDevice via testdsp::SteppedSine::transfer called through the DeviceUnderTest& abstract reference at 100/1000/10000 Hz; asserts every magnitude reads within 0.01 dB of 0 (true unity).
entries.why: Proves the L0 (lowest-level) stepped-sine ruler engine works when driven through virtual dispatch on the abstract base, not just via a concrete type — this is the polymorphism guarantee the whole runner architecture depends on.
entries.deviationMeans: Measuring through the abstract DeviceUnderTest& reference no longer produces correct results even for the simplest possible device — a fundamental break in the measurement-engine/interface boundary.

entries.key: DeviceUnderTest / real filter factories report TransferFunction / InputSweep
entries.what: Upcasts chz::makeMoogFut() and makeHuggettFut() to DeviceUnderTest& and asserts both report kind()==TransferFunction, excitation()==InputSweep, and correct names ("moog"/"huggett").
entries.why: Proves FilterUnderTest IS-A DeviceUnderTest for both real filter models — the upcast is the load-bearing relationship that lets CharacterizationRunner treat filters and reference/calibration devices uniformly.
entries.deviationMeans: A real filter's FilterUnderTest wrapper no longer satisfies the DeviceUnderTest contract correctly — code that generically dispatches on DeviceUnderTest& (like CharacterizationRunner::run) could misbehave for real filters specifically.

entries.key: DeviceUnderTest / runner accepts a DeviceUnderTest& (polymorphic)
entries.what: Passes a Moog FUT to CharacterizationRunner::run() typed strictly as DeviceUnderTest& (not FilterUnderTest&) with a 1-point grid, and asserts the resulting summary still contains the moog/LP24/fc1000/corner_hz key.
entries.why: End-to-end confirmation that the runner's public entry point works through the abstract base — this is what lets the same runner code serve filters, generators (GeneratorPathTests), and calibration devices (CaptureCalTests) without per-type overloads.
entries.deviationMeans: The runner no longer produces correct output when called through the abstract DeviceUnderTest& signature — any caller that type-erases to the base (which is most of the harness) would be affected even if concrete-type calls still work.

entries.key: SyntheticReference / ruler recovers an analytic biquad's exact response (<= 0.1 dB in-band)
entries.what: Measures an AnalyticBiquad (Q~5, fc=1000 Hz) via stepped-sine across 100 log-spaced probes 50-20kHz and asserts every measured magnitude matches the biquad's own analytic trueMagDb formula within 0.1 dB.
entries.why: An M2 trust gate: proves the ruler (stepped-sine measurement) recovers ground truth on a device whose exact answer is known by construction — tighter tolerance (0.1 dB) than the SteppedSineTests version since this is specifically the trust-gate for the measurement chain, not a method-comparison test.
entries.deviationMeans: The measurement ruler no longer recovers a known-exact analytic filter's response within the tight M2 trust tolerance — undermines confidence in every real-filter measurement built on the same ruler.

entries.key: SyntheticReference / peak-gain extractor matches the analytic peak (<= 0.2 dB)
entries.what: Compares testdsp::Level::peakGainDb applied to the measured response against the biquad's true analytic peak, asserting agreement within 0.2 dB.
entries.why: Validates the peak-extraction metric (used throughout the harness, e.g. CharacterizationGateTests' peak_gain_db golden) against a known-exact peak, not just the raw response curve.
entries.deviationMeans: The peak-gain extraction logic itself has a bug — even if the underlying response curve is measured correctly, the derived 'peak' summary metric used in golden checks and reports would be wrong.

entries.key: SyntheticReference / THD ruler recovers a cubic's closed-form THD (<= 0.5 dB)
entries.what: Measures THD of a pure-cubic nonlinearity (y=x+0.5x^3, which produces a pure 3rd harmonic) via testdsp::Harmonics::thdDb and compares against the closed-form trueThdDb (~-30.88 dB), asserting agreement within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: M2 trust gate for the THD measurement tool specifically (distinct from the MethodAgreementTests clean-passthrough THD-floor check) — proves it recovers an exact, non-trivial (nonzero) THD value from a device with a known closed-form answer.
entries.deviationMeans: The THD measurement tool doesn't recover a known, non-trivial distortion level accurately — any THD number reported for a real filter (e.g. the B3 distortion battery) would be suspect at the trust-gate level.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / peak / passband gain reductions over a response
entries.what: On a hand-built magnitude response { -3, 0, 6, 0, -12 } dB, asserts Level::peakGainDb returns 6.0 exactly, and passbandGainDb returns -3.0 for the Low passband and -12.0 for the High passband.
entries.why: Unit-level proof (exact, hand-computed answer) of the peak/passband extraction primitives that back headline metrics throughout the harness (e.g. RunnerLevelTests' peak_gain_db/passband_gain_db).
entries.deviationMeans: The peak or passband gain-extraction primitive is arithmetically wrong on a simple, hand-verifiable case — every derived metric using these primitives elsewhere in the suite would be suspect.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / dBFS on a DC signal is exact (0.5 -> -6.0206 dBFS, crest 0)
entries.what: On a constant 0.5-amplitude DC buffer, asserts peakDbfs and rmsDbfs both read -6.0206 dBFS (=20*log10(0.5)) within 0.01, and crestFactorDb reads 0.0 (peak==RMS for DC) within 0.01.
entries.why: Exact-answer unit proof of the basic level primitives (peak/RMS/crest) on the simplest possible signal (DC) where peak and RMS are mathematically identical — the foundation every other level measurement in the harness builds on.
entries.deviationMeans: The most basic peak/RMS/crest-factor calculations are wrong even on a trivial DC signal — every level-based measurement anywhere in the harness (which is most of it) would be suspect.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / crest factor of a sine is ~3.01 dB (sqrt(2))
entries.what: On a 1 kHz sine, asserts crestFactorDb reads 3.0103 dB (the exact peak-to-RMS ratio of a sine, sqrt(2)) within 0.2.
entries.why: Extends the DC-signal crest-factor proof to the more common (non-DC) case — a sine's exact theoretical crest factor is a well-known reference value used to sanity-check the metric on realistic audio.
entries.deviationMeans: The crest-factor calculation is wrong on a real (non-degenerate) waveform — any crest-factor-based diagnostic (e.g. MoogLadderTests' self-oscillation near-sine crest-factor check) would be unreliable.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / noise-floor RMS recovered (uniform amp 0.1 -> amp/sqrt(3))
entries.what: On uniform-distribution white noise (amplitude 0.1), asserts rmsDbfs matches the theoretical RMS of a uniform distribution (amp/sqrt(3), ~-24.77 dBFS) within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: Validates the RMS calculation against a known statistical-distribution closed form (uniform noise's RMS is analytically amp/sqrt(3)) — the noise-floor measurement building block used by RunnerNoiseFloorTests.
entries.deviationMeans: RMS measurement on noise-like (non-periodic) signals is inaccurate — the noise-floor measurement (used to catch a filter humming at idle) would be unreliable.

entries.key: LevelExtractors / empty input returns the -300 dB no-data sentinel (not silence/DC)
entries.what: Asserts peakGainDb (on an empty response vector) and peakDbfs/rmsDbfs/crestFactorDb (on an empty buffer) all return exactly -300.0 within 1e-9.
entries.why: The harness-wide -300 dB sentinel convention (also tested in AWeightingTests and LevelResponseTests) must hold consistently across every level primitive — this is effectively the canonical definition test for that convention, applied to the most foundational metrics.
entries.deviationMeans: One of the core level primitives is no longer returning the -300 sentinel for empty/no-data input — any harness code checking 'is this -300 (no data)' against these specific functions would misbehave, potentially treating missing data as a real (if oddly specific) measurement.

entries.key: RunnerLevel / summary records peak_gain_db and passband_gain_db (LP24, Moog)
entries.what: Runs Moog LP24 at max grid resonance (0.9) and asserts the summary contains peak_gain_db and passband_gain_db keys, both finite, with passband below -6 dB (authentic ladder bass loss at high resonance) and peak exceeding passband.
entries.why: This is the smallest assertion that fails if the runner stops recording absolute level at max resonance at all — the level axis was flagged as a HARD PREREQUISITE for real-hardware capture (SP-D) per project_filter_validation_harness's 2026-07-01 authenticity-purist decision.
entries.deviationMeans: The runner has stopped surfacing absolute level data at high resonance — this would silently regress the exact level-measurement capability that SP-D's hardware comparison depends on; note the -13 dB passband droop at res=0.9 is EXPECTED authentic Moog ladder behavior (per project memory), not itself a bug.

entries.key: RunnerProgress / runner reports monotonic progress ending at the announced total
entries.what: Runs a multi-mode/resonance/OS-factor grid (LP24+HP x res{0,0.9} x os{1,2}, 12 probes) with a progress-sink lambda; asserts the sink fires, announces a stable total from the first call, every subsequent 'done' strictly increases, the final done equals the announced total, every label is non-empty, and the summary is still correctly produced alongside the progress reporting.
entries.why: This is the exact runner-side contract for engagement item 6 (user, 2026-07-01: 'I want to see testing live, with progress bars or at least numbers on the screen') — the Progress sink referenced in this session's own recent commit history (de6bff5, 'chz live progress'); this contract is what any live-progress UI (dashboard, heavy-runner stderr status line) is built on top of.
entries.deviationMeans: The runner's progress reporting is no longer monotonic, doesn't end at the announced total, or has empty labels — any UI consuming this contract (the dashboard's live test view, or the heavy-runner stderr status line) would show glitchy, backwards, or incomplete progress, undermining the user-requested 'watch it running' feature this was built for.

entries.key: RunLog / writer emits well-formed throttled NDJSON and disables cleanly
entries.what: Verifies three runlog::Writer behaviors: (1) with throttle=0, every event (start/progress/test-with-escaped-quotes-and-newline/end) is written and each line round-trips through juce::JSON::parse correctly, including a 291-test end event; (2) with a huge throttle gap, mid-progress events are dropped but the final done==total progress is kept (3 lines instead of 5); (3) on POSIX, BERNIE_NO_RUNLOG=1 makes enabled() false.
entries.why: Franklin's dashboard (the Task 4 catalog's consumer, per the task brief) trusts .franklin/runs/*.ndjson files as its ONLY data source — this is literally the format this very task-4-brief.md Step 1 script parses to generate the catalog skeleton, so malformed JSON or a broken throttle would corrupt the exact pipeline this backfill task depends on.
entries.deviationMeans: Runlog files could become unparseable (breaking the dashboard AND this catalog's own generation script) or unboundedly large (no throttling) — treat any regression here as blocking for the whole Franklin dashboard effort, not just a DSP concern.

entries.key: LevelDisparity / Huggett resonant peak is far hotter than Moog (dense-sweep true disparity)
entries.what: Default-run marker (opt-in via BERNIE_RUN_DISPARITY, ~14 min/40000-probe sweep 10-25kHz): when run, measures Huggett's and Moog's true resonant peak gain at LP24/res=0.9 with a dense single-method stepped-sine sweep; asserts Huggett's peak lands in +15..+40 dB (bounded, post-Q27-fix), Moog's peak stays under +10 dB, and Huggett stays more than 10 dB hotter than Moog (character preserved).
entries.why: This is the harness that originally measured the pre-fix +88.97 dB Huggett peak (an 84 dB disparity vs Moog) documented in project_filter_validation_harness/project_device_char_framework — post-Q27-fix the same dense sweep is expected to land in a bounded band, distinguishing 'defect fixed' from 'character also accidentally removed'.
entries.deviationMeans: Either Huggett's peak has become unbounded again (>40 dB, Q27 regression) or has collapsed to Moog-like tameness (<15 dB, an over-correction that would erase the intentional Huggett-is-hotter character) — both are regressions, in opposite directions.

entries.key: AWeighting / curve matches IEC 61672 table anchors
entries.what: Checks AWeighting::aWeightDb against three IEC 61672 table anchors: 0 dB at 1 kHz (reference), -19.1 dB at 100 Hz, -2.5 dB at 10 kHz, each within 0.1-0.3 dB.
entries.why: The A-weighting lens is used throughout the Franklin harness (noise-floor, level-response) to report perceptually-relevant dB(A); if the curve itself is wrong every downstream A-weighted number is silently wrong.
entries.deviationMeans: The A-curve implementation has drifted from the published standard — any dB(A) figure reported elsewhere in the suite or dashboard is no longer trustworthy.

entries.key: AWeighting / single bin-aligned tone: weighted RMS = flat RMS + A(f)
entries.what: For bin-aligned tones at ~1 kHz (bin 341) and ~100 Hz (bin 34) in a 48 kHz/16384-sample buffer, asserts weighted RMS equals flat RMS + aWeightDb(f) within 0.1 dB (a single-bin Parseval identity).
entries.why: Confirms the RMS-domain weighting implementation is algebraically consistent with the per-frequency curve, not just correct at the anchor points.
entries.deviationMeans: The weighted-RMS computation and the per-frequency curve have diverged — likely a bug in how the curve is applied across a spectrum rather than in the curve values themselves.

entries.key: AWeighting / white noise: A-weighted RMS is below flat RMS
entries.what: Asserts that for broadband white noise, the A-weighted RMS is strictly lower than the flat (unweighted) RMS.
entries.why: Sanity check that the A-curve is net-attenuating over a typical audio spectrum (it de-emphasizes low and very-high frequencies), not accidentally net-boosting.
entries.deviationMeans: The A-weighting curve is providing net gain instead of attenuation on broadband content — a sign bug or curve inversion.

entries.key: AWeighting / empty input / f<=0 return the -300 sentinel
entries.what: Asserts an empty sample buffer and a non-positive frequency both return the -300 dB sentinel value (the harness convention for "no data"), not 0, NaN, or -inf.
entries.why: The -300 dB sentinel is a harness-wide convention (also used by LevelExtractors) for distinguishing 'no measurement' from a genuine near-silence reading; edge cases must not corrupt it into NaN which would break downstream comparisons/CSVs.
entries.deviationMeans: Degenerate inputs now produce NaN or a different sentinel — any harness code that filters on '-300 means no data' would start treating fake measurements as real, or crash on NaN propagation.

entries.key: AliasSplit / hand-built spectrum: exact below/above split
entries.what: Builds a 64-bin magnitude spectrum with a fundamental at bin 10, a harmonic at bin 20 (must be excluded from both halves), inharmonic energy below (bin 5) and above (bin 25) the fundamental; asserts Metrics::aliasSplit reports belowDb=10*log10(0.01) and aboveDb=10*log10(0.0025) exactly.
entries.why: aliasSplit is the audibility-relevant split between below-fundamental aliasing (perceived as dissonant/exposed) and above-fundamental aliasing (perceptually masked); harmonics of the fundamental must never be miscounted as alias energy in either half.
entries.deviationMeans: The alias-split accounting is off — either it is leaking harmonic energy into the below/above alias buckets, or the below/above boundary itself is wrong, corrupting every aliasing measurement built on it (e.g. CharacterizationRunner's OS-factor aliasing test).

entries.key: AliasSplit / EngineeredAliaser below the fundamental: split recovers 20log10(a/A)
entries.what: Injects a known alias tone below a bin-256 fundamental via chz::EngineeredAliaser (amplitude 0.01 vs fundamental 0.5) and asserts aliasSplit recovers belowDb = 20*log10(0.01/0.5) within 0.1 dB, with aboveDb below -60 dB (no leakage).
entries.why: End-to-end validation using a reference device with an analytically-known injected alias level, closing the gap between the hand-built-spectrum unit check and a realistic signal chain.
entries.deviationMeans: A known, precisely-placed below-fundamental alias tone is not being measured at its true level — the alias-split metric cannot be trusted for real (not hand-built) spectra.

entries.key: AliasSplit / EngineeredAliaser above the fundamental: split recovers it above
entries.what: Same EngineeredAliaser setup but with the alias tone at bin 700 (above the fundamental); asserts aboveDb recovers the known level within 0.1 dB and belowDb stays below -60 dB.
entries.why: Mirror-image check to the below-fundamental case, confirming the split boundary logic is symmetric and doesn't have a directional bug.
entries.deviationMeans: Above-fundamental alias energy is being misreported or is leaking into the below-fundamental bucket — the split has a directional asymmetry bug.

entries.key: LevelResponse / gain-vs-level matches the cubic's closed form
entries.what: Measures gain-vs-input-level (-40 to -1 dBFS) of a compressive CubicNonlinearity (c3=-0.3) and asserts small-signal gain is ~0 dB and gain at -6 dBFS matches the closed form 20*log10(1-0.225*A^2) = -0.503 dB within 0.2 dB.
entries.why: LevelResponse is the measurement tool used throughout the harness (HuggettBoundedResonanceTests, LargeSignalTests) to characterize gain-vs-level; this proves it against a device with an exact analytic closed-form answer before trusting it on real nonlinear filters.
entries.deviationMeans: The gain-vs-level measurement tool itself is wrong even on a device with a known closed-form answer — every gain-vs-level result elsewhere in the suite (including the Q27 bounded-resonance guard) would be unreliable.

entries.key: LevelResponse / THD-vs-level matches trueThdDb at the driven point
entries.what: At the -6 dBFS driven point, asserts LevelResponse's measured thdDb matches CubicNonlinearity's own trueThdDb() analytic value within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: Validates the THD side of LevelResponse's per-point measurement, not just gain — both are reported together (e.g. in LargeSignalTests' CSV rows) and must independently agree with ground truth.
entries.deviationMeans: THD measurement at a given input level no longer matches the analytically known value — any THD-vs-level curve reported by the harness (including large-signal drive-law reads) would be untrustworthy.

entries.key: LevelResponse / 1 dB compression knee at the closed-form input level
entries.what: Asserts LevelResponse::kneeInDbfs (the 1 dB compression point) matches the cubic's closed-form knee (-3.16 dBFS, derived from 1-0.225*A^2 = 10^(-1/20)) within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: The compression-knee metric is a headline characterization number (used in LevelDisparityTests-adjacent reporting); this proves it against an exact analytic answer.
entries.deviationMeans: The 1 dB compression knee detector no longer finds the correct input level — any reported compression-onset point for a real filter would be off.

entries.key: LevelResponse / knee sentinel when the device never compresses
entries.what: Measures a pure-unity (c3=0) device and asserts kneeInDbfs returns exactly the -300 dB sentinel (not a spurious found knee).
entries.why: A device that never compresses must report 'no knee found' via the harness-wide -300 sentinel convention, not a bogus in-range value — this is the negative-control counterpart to the positive knee-detection test.
entries.deviationMeans: The knee detector is finding a false compression point on a device that has none — downstream reporting could show a phantom compression knee for a linear device.

entries.key: LevelResponse / headroom-to-clip at the closed-form input level
entries.what: For an expansive cubic (c3=+0.5), asserts headroomToClipInDbfs matches the closed-form clip-onset level (-2.25 dBFS, where A+0.5*A^3=1.0 at A=0.7715) within 0.5 dB.
entries.why: Headroom-to-clip is the metric used to characterize how much level margin exists before a device hard-clips — validated here against an exact analytic answer for an expansive nonlinearity.
entries.deviationMeans: The headroom-to-clip detector no longer finds the correct clip-onset level for an expansive device — any reported headroom figure for a real filter/drive stage would be unreliable.

entries.key: LevelResponse / headroom sentinel when the ceiling is never reached
entries.what: Reusing the never-compressing linear device's points, asserts headroomToClipInDbfs also returns the -300 sentinel (no clip ceiling ever reached).
entries.why: Negative-control counterpart to the headroom-detection test — a device with no ceiling must report the sentinel, not a spurious in-range headroom value.
entries.deviationMeans: The headroom detector is reporting a phantom clip point for a device that never actually clips.

entries.key: GeneratorPath / CalibratedToneRef declares Generator / Trigger
entries.what: Asserts CalibratedToneRef, upcast to DeviceUnderTest&, reports kind()==Generator, excitation()==Trigger, and name()=="ref_tone".
entries.why: Generator/Trigger is the DeviceKind/Excitation pairing for devices that PRODUCE a known signal rather than respond to a swept input — this is the descriptor contract the runner uses to choose the Trigger driver instead of a sweep driver.
entries.deviationMeans: CalibratedToneRef's descriptor no longer identifies it as a Generator/Trigger device — the runner might try to sweep-excite it like a filter, which is meaningless for a fixed-level tone source.

entries.key: GeneratorPath / runner Trigger driver recovers the calibrated level
entries.what: Sets a -18 dBFS calibrated tone, runs it through CharacterizationRunner with the Trigger driver, and asserts peak_dbfs reads -18.0 (within 0.05), rms_dbfs reads -21.01 (within 0.05, the -3.01 dB sine peak-to-RMS ratio), rms_dbfsA matches rms_dbfs within 0.3 dB (A(1kHz)~0), and emission.csv is written.
entries.why: This is the Generator-path counterpart to the stepped-sine/ESS measurement paths — proves the runner's Trigger driver correctly measures a KNOWN, calibrated source (the building block CaptureCalTests' calibration-tone math and any future real-hardware level calibration depend on).
entries.deviationMeans: The runner's Trigger driver is misreporting a device's own known, calibrated signal level — any future calibration-tone-based capture step (SP-D) would be measuring from a broken baseline.

entries.key: CaptureCal / loopback calibration captures the chain response
entries.what: Calibrates a synthetic capture chain (8 kHz biquad tilt + -6.02 dB pad) via CaptureCal::calibrateChain across 100 log-spaced probes 50 Hz-20 kHz, then spot-checks that the recovered chain magnitude at the lowest probe (~100 Hz, where the biquad is flat) is -6.02 dB within 0.15 dB — i.e. just the pad.
entries.why: Loopback calibration (spec section 5.2) is the mechanism that will let a future real-hardware capture (SP-D/Summit) subtract out cable/interface/mic coloration; this proves the math on a chain with known, closed-form coloration before it's ever pointed at real hardware.
entries.deviationMeans: The loopback-calibration recovery is wrong even on a synthetic, perfectly-known chain — any real hardware capture (SP-D) built on this would misattribute chain coloration to the device under test.

entries.key: CaptureCal / compensation recovers the device's analytic response through the chain
entries.what: Measures a Q~5 biquad (fc=1000 Hz) observed THROUGH the calibrated synthetic chain, applies CaptureCal::compensate, and asserts the corrected response matches the device's true analytic magnitude within 0.2 dB at every probe.
entries.why: The end-to-end proof that calibration + compensation together recover ground truth even when the device itself has real (non-flat) coloration — the exact scenario SP-D needs for isolating the Summit's filter from its capture path.
entries.deviationMeans: Compensating a real device's measurement through a calibrated chain no longer removes the chain's coloration accurately — future hardware measurements (SP-D) would conflate capture-chain and device-under-test response.

entries.key: CaptureCal / calibration tone recovers the chain's level offset
entries.what: Feeds a -18 dBFS 1 kHz tone through just the pad (x0.5) and asserts CaptureCal::levelOffsetFromTone recovers exactly -6.02 dB (= 20*log10(0.5)) within 0.05 dB.
entries.why: A calibration TONE (as opposed to a full stepped-sine/ESS sweep) is the cheap, fast way to track just the level offset of a capture chain between full recalibrations — this is the arithmetic backbone of that shortcut.
entries.deviationMeans: The single-tone level-offset shortcut no longer matches a known pad gain — any code path using a calibration tone instead of a full sweep would misjudge the chain's gain.

entries.key: RunnerNoiseFloor / summary records noise_floor_dbfs + noise_floor_dbfsA (Moog LP24)
entries.what: Runs Moog LP24 at the base operating point (silence in) and asserts both noise_floor_dbfs (flat) and noise_floor_dbfsA (A-weighted) summary keys are present, are real data (not the -300 sentinel, i.e. > -300.5), and the flat value reads below -60 dBFS.
entries.why: Section 4.1 of the acceptance-criterion spec: idle-noise-floor measurement catches a filter that starts humming/leaking at idle — a regression a pure frequency-response measurement would never surface since it only tests driven behavior.
entries.deviationMeans: Either the noise-floor keys have stopped being recorded, or the filter now hums/leaks at idle above -60 dBFS — the latter would be an audible new idle noise the frequency-response-only gates (like CharacterizationGateTests) can't catch.

entries.key: VoicePerf / skipped (set BERNIE_RUN_VOICEPERF=1 to run the pricing measurement)
entries.what: Default-run marker: with BERNIE_RUN_VOICEPERF unset, only this skip beginTest registers (expect(true)); when set (and NOT a Debug build, which the test explicitly refuses per the 'perf only from Release' rule), it measures real Voice::render cost per {huggett,moog} x {light,heavy config} x OS factor {1,2,4,8} and prints a voices-per-core pricing table, plus an optional BERNIE_VOICEPERF_SECTIONS=1 per-stage (osc/up/shaper/spine/down) cost breakdown.
entries.why: This is the exact Q23 measurement mechanism — the register documents the hard-learned lesson that the first published pricing table was ~5-10x wrong because it ran on a Debug (-O0) build, which is why this test hard-refuses to print numbers under a Debug build (per the build rails' 'perf numbers ONLY from Release' rule) rather than silently producing wrong data again.
entries.deviationMeans: In normal CI, a failure only means the skip marker itself broke; to get real per-voice cost numbers (feeding the Q2 64-voice budget and Q11 per-phase CPU budgets), run BERNIE_RUN_VOICEPERF=1 from a genuine Release build — running it from Debug will correctly refuse to print misleading numbers rather than silently repeating the 2026-07-02 measurement-hygiene incident.

entries.key: RenderFingerprint / init_saw @ os1
entries.what: Renders the 'init_saw' reference patch (the plain saw voice through a wide-open (20kHz) Huggett LP with no drive/separation) through the real production Voice::render at OS factor 1, analyzes a ~0.68s (32768-sample) settled window, and golden-checks 10 octave-band RMS levels (31.25 Hz-16 kHz, 0.5 dB tol), peak dBFS (0.25 dB tol), and THD+N at the 110 Hz fundamental (1.0 dB tol) against tests/golden/fingerprints/baseline.
entries.why: Part of the anti-drift harness's 5-patch x 4-OS-factor sound fingerprint matrix (spec section 5) — covering every load-bearing signal path at the base (unoversampled) rate. The simplest possible reference patch (baseline saw, filter effectively bypassed) — a drift here means something as fundamental as the oscillator or amp envelope itself has changed, unrelated to any filter model.
entries.deviationMeans: The 'init_saw' patch's rendered sound has measurably drifted at os1 — since tolerances are musical (not sample-exact, denormal noise passes), a failure here means an audible voicing change, not just numeric noise; per the anti-drift harness convention, an INTENTIONAL change regenerates the golden via BERNIE_UPDATE_GOLDEN=1 with the diff justified in the commit message.

entries.why: Part of the anti-drift harness's 5-patch x 4-OS-factor sound fingerprint matrix (spec section 5) — covering every load-bearing signal path at 2x oversampling (mild anti-aliasing cost). entries.key: RenderFingerprint / hp_shaper @ os1

entries.key: LargeSignal / skipped (set BERNIE_RUN_LARGESIGNAL=1 for the SP-B large-signal read)
entries.what: Default-run marker: with BERNIE_RUN_LARGESIGNAL unset, this beginTest is the only one that registers in a normal suite run (a no-op expect(true)); the real content — a gain-vs-input-level sweep at the resonant peak for Huggett/Moog across resonances {0.7,0.9,1.0}, plus opt-in BERNIE_RUN_DROPOUT forensics reproducing the Q27 field report's 'sound disappears then re-appears' — only executes when the env var is set.
entries.why: This is the exact SP-B Phase 0 large-signal read that discovered the Q27 defect (Huggett gain rising +61 to +86 dB with input level vs Moog's textbook compression) — it is deliberately opt-in (heavy, ~minutes) per the build rails' 'opt-in heavy runs' convention, so routine CI only sees the skip marker.
entries.deviationMeans: A failure here (in normal CI) would only mean the skip-marker itself broke; to actually re-run the large-signal read and dropout forensics that informed the Q27 fix, set BERNIE_RUN_LARGESIGNAL=1 (and BERNIE_RUN_DROPOUT=1 for the forensics) locally.
